You are the generator in an offline, import-grade GCSE standard-deck compiler.

Create exactly 4 high-value recall cards for EVERY supplied selectable curriculum topic (40 cards total). Use only the official source excerpts below. The topic is a course-scope container: a card may use a narrower descendant concept, but topicComponentId must remain the supplied selectable topic id and curriculumComponentId must be the exact supplied descendant id whose excerpt supports the answer.

Hard rules:
- Output JSON only, matching the schema.
- This is AQA GCSE Chemistry; do not import facts from another board or version.
- Every answer must be entailed by one source excerpt. sourceExcerpt must be ONE short passage of at most 400 characters and a character-for-character CONTIGUOUS substring copied from the selected component's exact text value. Prefer one sentence, clause or bullet. Preserve every space, line break, punctuation mark and character at that location. Never copy equation/diagram layout, reflow a line, insert a line break, join paragraphs or non-contiguous fragments, omit words or clauses from the middle, use ellipses, or emit control bytes. Before returning JSON, verify mentally that source.text.includes(sourceExcerpt) would be true. Give that component id and its page locator.
- Preserve source qualifiers exactly: do not turn "may", "typical", "without knowledge of", a listed sequence, or a limited example into a stronger universal, credential rule, or spatial convention.
- Exclude Higher-only content whenever ANY target offering is Foundation. Prefer shared core knowledge, never silently map Higher-only content to Foundation.
- Fronts must support unaided recall and be self-contained: never use unresolved phrases such as "these issues". Backs are concise canonical answers. Write a separate explanation. memoryTip may be null and should be null when no honest, non-contrived retrieval route exists; never restate the answer or force a gimmick. Every factual contrast or clause in the explanation must be directly supported by the cited excerpt; omit even generally true embellishment when the excerpt does not state it.
- Use one meaningful emoji from the supplied allowlist. It must orient without revealing the answer or a defining property of the answer. Do not use a generic book/note cue merely to mean "study". For security, encryption, lock, key or access questions, do not use 🔐.
- Supply exactly three or four unique choices. Exactly one isCorrect=true and its text equals back exactly. Use four total choices only when there are three distinct, plausible neighbouring misconceptions; use three total choices when a third distractor would be contrived or repetitive. Every choice has concise teaching feedback and every wrong choice has a specific misconception label. Do not make distractors by combining unrelated nonsense categories.
- Prefer durable definitions, relationships, sequence, comparison, methods and exact governing facts over trivia. Vary kinds within each topic.
- A card must retrieve a usable subject concept or method. Do not turn an assessment objective, curriculum heading, list of command verbs, or paired near-synonyms into content merely because it appears in the specification.
- IDs, concept keys and all choice keys are lowercase kebab-case and globally specific. Use stable choice keys a, b, c and d in displayed order. Do not mention answer controls in the front.
- Do not create quotation or plot cards from curriculum specifications.

Allowed kinds: definition, formula, process, test-result, unit, practical, fact, comparison, plot, quotation, character, theme, context, method, case-study, chronology, cause-consequence, interpretation, technique, structure.
Allowed emoji for Chemistry: ⚛️ 🔗 ⚖️ 🧪 🧂 🔥 ⚡ 🧊 ⏱️ ⛽ 📏 🫧 ♻️ 🌡️.

          "text": "Official PDF pages 17-26\nAtomic structure and the periodic table\nThe periodic table provides chemists with a structured organisation of the known chemical\nelements from which they can make sense of their physical and chemical properties. The historical\ndevelopment of the periodic table and models of atomic structure provide good examples of how\nscientific ideas and explanations develop over time as new evidence emerges. The arrangement of\nelements in the modern periodic table can be explained in terms of atomic structure which provides\nevidence for the model of a nuclear atom with electrons in energy levels.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 17\n4.1.1 A simple model of the atom, symbols, relative atomic mass,\nelectronic charge and isotopes\n4.1.1.1 Atoms, elements and compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAll substances are made of atoms. An atom is the smallest part of\nan element that can exist.\nAtoms of each element are represented by a chemical symbol, eg\nO represents an atom of oxygen, Na represents an atom of sodium.\nThere are about 100 different elements. Elements are shown in the\nperiodic table.\nCompounds are formed from elements by chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions always involve the formation of one or more\nnew substances, and often involve a detectable energy change.\nCompounds contain two or more elements chemically combined in\nfixed proportions and can be represented by formulae using the\nsymbols of the atoms from which they were formed. Compounds\ncan only be separated into elements by chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be represented by word equations or\nequations using symbols and formulae.\nStudents will be supplied with a periodic table for the exam and\nshould be able to:\n• use the names and symbols of the first 20 elements in the\nperiodic table, the elements in Groups 1 and 7, and other\nelements in this specification\n• name compounds of these elements from given formulae or\nsymbol equations\n• write word equations for the reactions in this specification\n• write formulae and balanced chemical equations for the\nreactions in this specification.\n(HT only) write balanced half equations and ionic equations where\nappropriate.\n18 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.1.2 Mixtures\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nA mixture consists of two or more elements or compounds not\nchemically combined together. The chemical properties of each\nsubstance in the mixture are unchanged.\nMixtures can be separated by physical processes such as filtration,\ncrystallisation, simple distillation, fractional distillation and\nchromatography. These physical processes do not involve chemical\nreactions and no new substances are made.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe, explain and give examples of the specified\nprocesses of separation\n• suggest suitable separation and purification techniques for\nmixtures when given appropriate information.\nWS 2.2, 2.3\nAT 4\nSafe use of a range of\nequipment to separate\nchemical mixtures.\n4.1.1.3 The development of the model of the atom (common content with physics)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNew experimental evidence may lead to a scientific model being\nchanged or replaced.\nBefore the discovery of the electron, atoms were thought to be tiny\nspheres that could not be divided.\nThe discovery of the electron led to the plum pudding model of the\natom. The plum pudding model suggested that the atom is a ball of\npositive charge with negative electrons embedded in it.\nThe results from the alpha particle scattering experiment led to the\nconclusion that the mass of an atom was concentrated at the centre\n(nucleus) and that the nucleus was charged. This nuclear model\nreplaced the plum pudding model.\nNiels Bohr adapted the nuclear model by suggesting that electrons\norbit the nucleus at specific distances. The theoretical calculations\nof Bohr agreed with experimental observations.\nLater experiments led to the idea that the positive charge of any\nnucleus could be subdivided into a whole number of smaller\nparticles, each particle having the same amount of positive charge.\nThe name proton was given to these particles.\nThe experimental work of James Chadwick provided the evidence\nto show the existence of neutrons within the nucleus. This was\nabout 20 years after the nucleus became an accepted scientific\nidea.\nStudents should be able to describe:\nWS 1.1, 1.6\nThis historical context\nprovides an opportunity for\nstudents to show an\nunderstanding of why and\ndescribe how scientific\nmethods and theories\ndevelop over time.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 19\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\n• why the new evidence from the scattering experiment led to a\nchange in the atomic model\nWS 1.1\n• the difference between the plum pudding model of the atom\nand the nuclear model of the atom.\nWS 1.2\nDetails of experimental work supporting the Bohr model are not\nrequired.\nDetails of Chadwick’s experimental work are not required.\n4.1.1.4 Relative electrical charges of subatomic particles\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative electrical charges of the particles in atoms are:\nIn an atom, the number of electrons is equal to the number of\nprotons in the nucleus. Atoms have no overall electrical charge.\nThe number of protons in an atom of an element is its atomic\nnumber. All atoms of a particular element have the same number of\nprotons. Atoms of different elements have different numbers of\nprotons.\nStudents should be able to use the nuclear model to describe\natoms.\nWS 1.2\n20 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.1.5 Size and mass of atoms\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAtoms are very small, having a radius of about 0.1 nm (1 x 10-10 m).\nThe radius of a nucleus is less than 1/10 000 of that of the atom\n(about 1 x 10-14 m).\nAlmost all of the mass of an atom is in the nucleus.\nThe relative masses of protons, neutrons and electrons are:\nThe sum of the protons and neutrons in an atom is its mass\nnumber.\nAtoms of the same element can have different numbers of\nneutrons; these atoms are called isotopes of that element.\nAtoms can be represented as shown in this example:\nStudents should be able to calculate the numbers of protons,\nneutrons and electrons in an atom or ion, given its atomic number\nand mass number.\nWS 4.3, 4\nUse SI units and the prefix\nnano.\nMS 1b\nRecognise expressions in\nstandard form.\nStudents should be able to relate size and scale of atoms to objects\nin the physical world.\nMS 1d\n4.1.1.6 Relative atomic mass\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relat"

The electronic structure of an atom can\nbe represented by numbers or by a diagram. Atoms have no overall electrical charge.\nThe number of protons in an atom of an element is its atomic\nnumber. All atoms of a particular element have the same number of\nprotons. Atoms of different elements have different numbers of\nprotons.\nStudents should be able to use the nuclear model to describe\natoms.\nWS 1.2\n20 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.1.5 Size and mass of atoms\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAtoms are very small, having a radius of about 0.1 nm (1 x 10-10 m).\nThe radius of a nucleus is less than 1/10 000 of that of the atom\n(about 1 x 10-14 m).\nAlmost all of the mass of an atom is in the nucleus.\nThe relative masses of protons, neutrons and electrons are:\nThe sum of the protons and neutrons in an atom is its mass\nnumber.\nAtoms of the same element can have different numbers of\nneutrons; these atoms are called isotopes of that element.\nAtoms can be represented as shown in this example:\nStudents should be able to calculate the numbers of protons,\nneutrons and electrons in an atom or ion, given its atomic number\nand mass number.\nWS 4.3, 4\nUse SI units and the prefix\nnano.\nMS 1b\nRecognise expressions in\nstandard form.\nStudents should be able to relate size and scale of atoms to objects\nin the physical world.\nMS 1d\n4.1.1.6 Relative atomic mass\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative atomic mass of an element is an average value that\ntakes account of the abundance of the isotopes of the element.\nStudents should be able to calculate the relative atomic mass of an\nelement given the percentage abundance of its isotopes.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 21\n4.1.1.7 Electronic structure\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe electrons in an atom occupy the lowest available energy levels\n(innermost available shells). The electronic structure of an atom can\nbe represented by numbers or by a diagram. For example, the\nelectronic structure of sodium is 2,8,1 or\nshowing two electrons in the lowest energy level, eight in the\nsecond energy level and one in the third energy level.\nStudents may answer questions in terms of either energy levels or\nshells.\nWS 1.2\nStudents should be able to\nrepresent the electronic\nstructures of the first twenty\nelements of the periodic\ntable in both forms.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n4.1.2 The periodic table\n4.1.2.1 The periodic table\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in the periodic table are arranged in order of atomic\n(proton) number and so that elements with similar properties are in\ncolumns, known as groups. The table is called a periodic table\nbecause similar properties occur at regular intervals.\nElements in the same group in the periodic table have the same\nnumber of electrons in their outer shell (outer electrons) and this\ngives them similar chemical properties.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how the position of an element in the periodic table is\nrelated to the arrangement of electrons in its atoms and hence\nto its atomic number\n• predict possible reactions and probable reactivity of elements\nfrom their positions in the periodic table.\nWS 1.2\n22 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.2.2 Development of the periodic table\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nBefore the discovery of protons, neutrons and electrons, scientists\nattempted to classify the elements by arranging them in order of\ntheir atomic weights.\nThe early periodic tables were incomplete and some elements were\nplaced in inappropriate gro"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 17-26\nThe development of the model of the atom (common content with physics)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNew experimental evidence may lead to a scientific model being\nchanged or replaced.\nBefore the discovery of the electron, atoms were thought to be tiny\nspheres that could not be divided.\nThe discovery of the electron led to the plum pudding model of the\natom. The plum pudding model suggested that the atom is a ball of\npositive charge with negative electrons embedded in it.\nThe results from the alpha particle scattering experiment led to the\nconclusion that the mass of an atom was concentrated at the centre\n(nucleus) and that the nucleus was charged. This nuclear model\nreplaced the plum pudding model.\nNiels Bohr adapted the nuclear model by suggesting that electrons\norbit the nucleus at specific distances. The theoretical calculations\nof Bohr agreed with experimental observations.\nLater experiments led to the idea that the positive charge of any\nnucleus could be subdivided into a whole number of smaller\nparticles, each particle having the same amount of positive charge.\nThe name proton was given to these particles.\nThe experimental work of James Chadwick provided the evidence\nto show the existence of neutrons within the nucleus. This was\nabout 20 years after the nucleus became an accepted scientific\nidea.\nStudents should be able to describe:\nWS 1.1, 1.6\nThis historical context\nprovides an opportunity for\nstudents to show an\nunderstanding of why and\ndescribe how scientific\nmethods and theories\ndevelop over time.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. The table is called a periodic table\nbecause similar properties occur at regular intervals.\nElements in the same group in the periodic table have the same\nnumber of electrons in their outer shell (outer electrons) and this\ngives them similar chemical properties.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how the position of an element in the periodic table is\nrelated to the arrangement of electrons in its atoms and hence\nto its atomic number\n• predict possible reactions and probable reactivity of elements\nfrom their positions in the periodic table.\nWS 1.2\n22 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.2.2 Development of the periodic table\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nBefore the discovery of protons, neutrons and electrons, scientists\nattempted to classify the elements by arranging them in order of\ntheir atomic weights.\nThe early periodic tables were incomplete and some elements were\nplaced in inappropriate groups if the strict order of atomic weights\nwas followed.\nMendeleev overcame some of the problems by leaving gaps for\nelements that he thought had not been discovered and in some\nplaces changed the order based on atomic weights.\nElements with properties predicted by Mendeleev were discovered\nand filled the gaps. Knowledge of isotopes made it possible to\nexplain why the order based on atomic weights was not always\ncorrect.\nStudents should be able to describe these steps in the development\nof the periodic table.\nWS 1.1, 1.6\nExplain how testing a\nprediction can support or\nrefute a new scientific idea.\n4.1.2.3 Metals and non-metals\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nElements that react to form positive ions are metals.\nElements that do not form p"

Metals are found to the left\nand towards the bottom of the periodic table. Non-metals are found\ntowards the right and top of the periodic table.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain the differences between metals and non-metals on the\nbasis of their characteristic physical and chemical properties.\nThis links to Group 0 (page 24), Group 1 (page 24), Group\n7 (page 25) and Bonding, structure and the properties of\nmatter (page 26)\n• explain how the atomic structure of metals and non-metals\nrelates to their position in the periodic table\n• explain how the reactions of elements are related to the\narrangement of electrons in their atoms and hence to their\natomic number.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. They are unreactive and do not easily form molecules\nbecause their atoms have stable arrangements of electrons. The\nnoble gases have eight electrons in their outer shell, except for\nhelium, which has only two electrons.\nThe boiling points of the noble gases increase with increasing\nrelative atomic mass (going down the group).\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how properties of the elements in Group 0 depend on\nthe outer shell of electrons of the atoms\n• predict properties from given trends down the group.\nWS 1.2\n4.1.2.5 Group 1\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in Group 1 of the periodic table are known as the\nalkali metals and have characteristic properties because of the\nsingle electron in their outer shell.\nStudents should be able to describe the reactions of the first three\nalkali metals with oxygen, chlorine and water.\nIn Group 1, the reactivity of the elements increases going down the\ngroup.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how properties of the elements in Group 1 depend on\nthe outer shell of electrons of the a"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 17-26\nrelative atomic mass,\nelectronic charge and isotopes\n4.1.1.1 Atoms, elements and compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAll substances are made of atoms. An atom is the smallest part of\nan element that can exist.\nAtoms of each element are represented by a chemical symbol, eg\nO represents an atom of oxygen, Na represents an atom of sodium.\nThere are about 100 different elements. Elements are shown in the\nperiodic table.\nCompounds are formed from elements by chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions always involve the formation of one or more\nnew substances, and often involve a detectable energy change.\nCompounds contain two or more elements chemically combined in\nfixed proportions and can be represented by formulae using the\nsymbols of the atoms from which they were formed. Compounds\ncan only be separated into elements by chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be represented by word equations or\nequations using symbols and formulae.\nStudents will be supplied with a periodic table for the exam and\nshould be able to:\n• use the names and symbols of the first 20 elements in the\nperiodic table, the elements in Groups 1 and 7, and other\nelements in this specification\n• name compounds of these elements from given formulae or\nsymbol equations\n• write word equations for the reactions in this specification\n• write formulae and balanced chemical equations for the\nreactions in this specification.\n(HT only) write balanced half equations and ionic equations where\nappropriate.\n18 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.1.2 Mixtures\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nA mixture consists of two or more elements or compounds not\nchemically combined together. The chemical properties of each\nsubstance in the mixture are unchanged.\nMixtures can be separated by physical processes such as filtration,\ncrystallisation, simple distillation, fractional distillation and\nchromatography. These physical processes do not involve chemical\nreactions and no new substances are made.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe, explain and give examples of the specified\nprocesses of separation\n• suggest suitable separation and purification techniques for\nmixtures when given appropriate information.\nWS 2.2, 2.3\nAT 4\nSafe use of a range of\nequipment to separate\nchemical mixtures.\n4.1.1.3 The development of the model of the atom (common content with physics)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNew experimental evidence may lead to a scientific model being\nchanged or replaced.\nBefore the discovery of the electron, atoms were thought to be tiny\nspheres that could not be divided.\nThe discovery of the electron led to the plum pudding model of the\natom. The plum pudding model suggested that the atom is a ball of\npositive charge with negative electrons embedded in it.\nThe results from the alpha particle scattering experiment led to the\nconclusion that the mass of an atom was concentrated at the centre\n(nucleus) and that the nucleus was charged. This nuclear model\nreplaced the plum pudding model.\nNiels Bohr adapted the nuclear model by suggesting that electrons\norbit the nucleus at specific distances. The theoretical calculations\nof Bohr agreed with experimental observations.\nLater experiments led to the idea that the positive charge of any\nnucleus could be subdivided into a whole number of smaller\nparticles, each particle having the same amount of positive charge.\nThe name proton was given to these particles.\nThe experimental work of James Chadwick provided the evidence\nto show the existence of neutrons within the nucleus. This was\nabout 20 years after the nucleus became an accepted scientific\nidea.\nStudents should be able to describe:\nWS 1.1, 1.6\nThis historical context\nprovides an opportunity for\nstudents to show an\nunderstanding of why and\ndescribe how scientific\nmethods and theories\ndevelop over time.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 19\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\n• why the new evidence from the scattering experiment led to a\nchange in the atomic model\nWS 1.1\n• the difference between the plum pudding model of the atom\nand the nuclear model of the atom.\nWS 1.2\nDetails of experimental work supporting the Bohr model are not\nrequired.\nDetails of Chadwick’s experimental work are not required.\n4.1.1.4 Relative electrical charges of subatomic particles\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative electrical charges of the particles in atoms are:\nIn an atom, the number of electrons is equal to the number of\nprotons in the nucleus. Atoms have no overall electrical charge.\nThe number of protons in an atom of an element is its atomic\nnumber. All atoms of a particular element have the same number of\nprotons. Atoms of different elements have different numbers of\nprotons.\nStudents should be able to use the nuclear model to describe\natoms.\nWS 1.2\n20 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.1.5 Size and mass of atoms\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAtoms are very small, having a radius of about 0.1 nm (1 x 10-10 m).\nThe radius of a nucleus is less than 1/10 000 of that of the atom\n(about 1 x 10-14 m).\nAlmost all of the mass of an atom is in the nucleus.\nThe relative masses of protons, neutrons and electrons are:\nThe sum of the protons and neutrons in an atom is its mass\nnumber.\nAtoms of the same element can have different numbers of\nneutrons; these atoms are called isotopes of that element.\nAtoms can be represented as shown in this example:\nStudents should be able to calculate the numbers of protons,\nneutrons and electrons in an atom or ion, given its atomic number\nand mass number.\nWS 4.3, 4\nUse SI units and the prefix\nnano.\nMS 1b\nRecognise expressions in\nstandard form.\nStudents should be able to relate size and scale of atoms to objects\nin the physical world.\nMS 1d\n4.1.1.6 Relative atomic mass\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative atomic mass of an element is an average value that\ntakes account of the abundance of the isotopes of the element.\nStudents should be able to calculate the relative atomic mass of an\nelement given the percentage abundance of its isotopes.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 21\n4.1.1.7 Electronic structure\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe electrons in an atom occupy the lowest available energy levels\n(innermost available shells). The electronic structure of an atom can\nbe represented by numbers or by a diagram. For example, the\nelectronic structure of sodium is 2,8,1 or\nshowing two electrons in the lowest energy level, eight in the\nsecond ener"

          "id": "aqa-gcse-chemistry-8462-v1.1:4-1-1-7",

          "text": "Official PDF pages 17-26\nElectronic structure\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe electrons in an atom occupy the lowest available energy levels\n(innermost available shells). The electronic structure of an atom can\nbe represented by numbers or by a diagram. For example, the\nelectronic structure of sodium is 2,8,1 or\nshowing two electrons in the lowest energy level, eight in the\nsecond energy level and one in the third energy level.\nStudents may answer questions in terms of either energy levels or\nshells.\nWS 1.2\nStudents should be able to\nrepresent the electronic\nstructures of the first twenty\nelements of the periodic\ntable in both forms.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n4.1.2 The periodic table\n4.1.2.1 The periodic table\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in the periodic table are arranged in order of atomic\n(proton) number and so that elements with similar properties are in\ncolumns, known as groups. The table is called a periodic table\nbecause similar properties occur at regular intervals.\nElements in the same group in the periodic table have the same\nnumber of electrons in their outer shell (outer electrons) and this\ngives them similar chemical properties.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how the position of an element in the periodic table is\nrelated to the arrangement of electrons in its atoms and hence\nto its atomic number\n• predict possible reactions and probable reactivity of elements\nfrom their positions in the periodic table.\nWS 1.2\n22 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.2.2 Development of the periodic table\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nBefore the discovery of protons, neutrons and electrons, scientists\nattempted to classify the elements by arranging them in order of\ntheir atomic weights.\nThe early periodic tables were incomplete and some elements were\nplaced in inappropriate groups if the strict order of atomic weights\nwas followed.\nMendeleev overcame some of the problems by leaving gaps for\nelements that he thought had not been discovered and in some\nplaces changed the order based on atomic weights.\nElements with properties predicted by Mendeleev were discovered\nand filled the gaps. Knowledge of isotopes made it possible to\nexplain why the order based on atomic weights was not always\ncorrect.\nStudents should be able to describe these steps in the development\nof the periodic table.\nWS 1.1, 1.6\nExplain how testing a\nprediction can support or\nrefute a new scientific idea.\n4.1.2.3 Metals and non-metals\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nElements that react to form positive ions are metals.\nElements that do not form positive ions are non-metals.\nThe majority of elements are metals. Metals are found to the left\nand towards the bottom of the periodic table. Non-metals are found\ntowards the right and top of the periodic table.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain the differences between metals and non-metals on the\nbasis of their characteristic physical and chemical properties.\nThis links to Group 0 (page 24), Group 1 (page 24), Group\n7 (page 25) and Bonding, structure and the properties of\nmatter (page 26)\n• explain how the atomic structure of metals and non-metals\nrelates to their position in the periodic table\n• explain how the reactions of elements are related to the\narrangement of electrons in their atoms and hence to their\natomic number.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 23\n4.1.2.4 Group 0\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in Group 0 of the periodic table are called the noble\ngases. They are unreactive and do not easily form molecules\nbecause their atoms have stable arrangements of electrons. The\nnoble gases have eight electrons in their outer shell, except for\nhelium, which has only two electrons.\nThe boiling points of the noble gases increase with increasing\nrelative atomic mass (going down the group).\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how properties of the elements in Group 0 depend on\nthe outer shell of electrons of the atoms\n• predict properties from given trends down the group.\nWS 1.2\n4.1.2.5 Group 1\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in Group 1 of the periodic table are known as the\nalkali metals and have characteristic properties because of the\nsingle electron in their outer shell.\nStudents should be able to describe the reactions of the first three\nalkali metals with oxygen, chlorine and water.\nIn Group 1, the reactivity of the elements increases going down the\ngroup.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how properties of the elements in Group 1 depend on\nthe outer shell of electrons of the atoms\n• predict properties from given trends down the group.\nWS 1.2\n24 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.2.6 Group 7\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in Group 7 of the periodic table are known as the\nhalogens and have similar reactions because they all have seven\nelectrons in their outer shell. The halogens are non-metals and\nconsist of molecules made of pairs of atoms.\nStudents should be able to describe the nature of the compounds\nformed when chlorine, bromine and iodine react with metals and\nnon-metals.\nIn Group 7, the further down the group an element is the higher its\nrelative molecular mass, melting point and boiling point.\nIn Group 7, the reactivity of the elements decreases going down the\ngroup.\nA more reactive halogen can displace a less reactive halogen from\nan aqueous solution of its salt.\nAT 6\nOffers an opportunity within\ndisplacement reactions of\nhalogens.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how properties of the elements in Group 7 depend on\nthe outer shell of electrons of the atoms\n• predict properties from given trends down the group.\nWS 1.2\n4.1.3 Properties of transition metals (chemistry only)\n4.1.3.1 Comparison with Group 1 elements\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe transition elements are metals with similar properties which are\ndifferent from those of the elements in Group 1.\nStudents should be able to describe the difference compared with\nGroup 1 in melting points, densities, strength, hardness and\nreactivity with oxygen, water and halogens.\nStudents should be able to exemplify these general properties by\nreference to Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 25\n4.1.3.2 Typical properties\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMany transition elements have ions with different charges, form\ncoloured compounds and are useful as catalysts.\nStudents should be ab"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 17-26\nthe periodic table\nThe periodic table provides chemists with a structured organisation of the known chemical\nelements from which they can make sense of their physical and chemical properties. The historical\ndevelopment of the periodic table and models of atomic structure provide good examples of how\nscientific ideas and explanations develop over time as new evidence emerges. The arrangement of\nelements in the modern periodic table can be explained in terms of atomic structure which provides\nevidence for the model of a nuclear atom with electrons in energy levels.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 17\n4.1.1 A simple model of the atom, symbols, relative atomic mass,\nelectronic charge and isotopes\n4.1.1.1 Atoms, elements and compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAll substances are made of atoms. An atom is the smallest part of\nan element that can exist.\nAtoms of each element are represented by a chemical symbol, eg\nO represents an atom of oxygen, Na represents an atom of sodium.\nThere are about 100 different elements. Elements are shown in the\nperiodic table.\nCompounds are formed from elements by chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions always involve the formation of one or more\nnew substances, and often involve a detectable energy change.\nCompounds contain two or more elements chemically combined in\nfixed proportions and can be represented by formulae using the\nsymbols of the atoms from which they were formed. Compounds\ncan only be separated into elements by chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be represented by word equations or\nequations using symbols and formulae.\nStudents will be supplied with a periodic table for the exam and\nshould be able to:\n• use the names and symbols of the first 20 elements in the\nperiodic table, the elements in Groups 1 and 7, and other\nelements in this specification\n• name compounds of these elements from given formulae or\nsymbol equations\n• write word equations for the reactions in this specification\n• write formulae and balanced chemical equations for the\nreactions in this specification.\n(HT only) write balanced half equations and ionic equations where\nappropriate.\n18 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.1.2 Mixtures\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nA mixture consists of two or more elements or compounds not\nchemically combined together. The chemical properties of each\nsubstance in the mixture are unchanged.\nMixtures can be separated by physical processes such as filtration,\ncrystallisation, simple distillation, fractional distillation and\nchromatography. These physical processes do not involve chemical\nreactions and no new substances are made.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe, explain and give examples of the specified\nprocesses of separation\n• suggest suitable separation and purification techniques for\nmixtures when given appropriate information.\nWS 2.2, 2.3\nAT 4\nSafe use of a range of\nequipment to separate\nchemical mixtures.\n4.1.1.3 The development of the model of the atom (common content with physics)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNew experimental evidence may lead to a scientific model being\nchanged or replaced.\nBefore the discovery of the electron, atoms were thought to be tiny\nspheres that could not be divided.\nThe discovery of the electron led to the plum pudding model of the\natom. The plum pudding model suggested that the atom is a ball of\npositive charge with negative electrons embedded in it.\nThe results from the alpha particle scattering experiment led to the\nconclusion that the mass of an atom was concentrated at the centre\n(nucleus) and that the nucleus was charged. This nuclear model\nreplaced the plum pudding model.\nNiels Bohr adapted the nuclear model by suggesting that electrons\norbit the nucleus at specific distances. The theoretical calculations\nof Bohr agreed with experimental observations.\nLater experiments led to the idea that the positive charge of any\nnucleus could be subdivided into a whole number of smaller\nparticles, each particle having the same amount of positive charge.\nThe name proton was given to these particles.\nThe experimental work of James Chadwick provided the evidence\nto show the existence of neutrons within the nucleus. This was\nabout 20 years after the nucleus became an accepted scientific\nidea.\nStudents should be able to describe:\nWS 1.1, 1.6\nThis historical context\nprovides an opportunity for\nstudents to show an\nunderstanding of why and\ndescribe how scientific\nmethods and theories\ndevelop over time.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 19\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\n• why the new evidence from the scattering experiment led to a\nchange in the atomic model\nWS 1.1\n• the difference between the plum pudding model of the atom\nand the nuclear model of the atom.\nWS 1.2\nDetails of experimental work supporting the Bohr model are not\nrequired.\nDetails of Chadwick’s experimental work are not required.\n4.1.1.4 Relative electrical charges of subatomic particles\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative electrical charges of the particles in atoms are:\nIn an atom, the number of electrons is equal to the number of\nprotons in the nucleus. Atoms have no overall electrical charge.\nThe number of protons in an atom of an element is its atomic\nnumber. All atoms of a particular element have the same number of\nprotons. Atoms of different elements have different numbers of\nprotons.\nStudents should be able to use the nuclear model to describe\natoms.\nWS 1.2\n20 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.1.5 Size and mass of atoms\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAtoms are very small, having a radius of about 0.1 nm (1 x 10-10 m).\nThe radius of a nucleus is less than 1/10 000 of that of the atom\n(about 1 x 10-14 m).\nAlmost all of the mass of an atom is in the nucleus.\nThe relative masses of protons, neutrons and electrons are:\nThe sum of the protons and neutrons in an atom is its mass\nnumber.\nAtoms of the same element can have different numbers of\nneutrons; these atoms are called isotopes of that element.\nAtoms can be represented as shown in this example:\nStudents should be able to calculate the numbers of protons,\nneutrons and electrons in an atom or ion, given its atomic number\nand mass number.\nWS 4.3, 4\nUse SI units and the prefix\nnano.\nMS 1b\nRecognise expressions in\nstandard form.\nStudents should be able to relate size and scale of atoms to objects\nin the physical world.\nMS 1d\n4.1.1.6 Relative atomic mass\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative atomic mass of an element is an average value that\ntakes account of the abundance of the isotopes of the element.\nStudents should be able to calculate the relative atomic mass of an\nelement given the percentage abundance of"

          "id": "aqa-gcse-chemistry-8462-v1.1:4-1-2-3",
          "code": "4.1.2.3",
          "title": "Metals and non-metals",
          "kind": "topic",
          "tier": [
            "Foundation",
            "Higher"
          ],
          "locator": "Official PDF pages 17-26; 4.1.2.3 Metals and non-metals",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 17-26\nMetals and non-metals\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nElements that react to form positive ions are metals.\nElements that do not form positive ions are non-metals.\nThe majority of elements are metals. Metals are found to the left\nand towards the bottom of the periodic table. Non-metals are found\ntowards the right and top of the periodic table.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain the differences between metals and non-metals on the\nbasis of their characteristic physical and chemical properties.\nThis links to Group 0 (page 24), Group 1 (page 24), Group\n7 (page 25) and Bonding, structure and the properties of\nmatter (page 26)\n• explain how the atomic structure of metals and non-metals\nrelates to their position in the periodic table\n• explain how the reactions of elements are related to the\narrangement of electrons in their atoms and hence to their\natomic number.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 23\n4.1.2.4 Group 0\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in Group 0 of the periodic table are called the noble\ngases. They are unreactive and do not easily form molecules\nbecause their atoms have stable arrangements of electrons. The\nnoble gases have eight electrons in their outer shell, except for\nhelium, which has only two electrons.\nThe boiling points of the noble gases increase with increasing\nrelative atomic mass (going down the group).\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how properties of the elements in Group 0 depend on\nthe outer shell of electrons of the atoms\n• predict properties from given trends down the group.\nWS 1.2\n4.1.2.5 Group 1\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in Group 1 of the periodic table are known as the\nalkali metals and have characteristic properties because of the\nsingle electron in their outer shell.\nStudents should be able to describe the reactions of the first three\nalkali metals with oxygen, chlorine and water.\nIn Group 1, the reactivity of the elements increases going down the\ngroup.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how properties of the elements in Group 1 depend on\nthe outer shell of electrons of the atoms\n• predict properties from given trends down the group.\nWS 1.2\n24 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.1.2.6 Group 7\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe elements in Group 7 of the periodic table are known as the\nhalogens and have similar reactions because they all have seven\nelectrons in their outer shell. The halogens are non-metals and\nconsist of molecules made of pairs of atoms.\nStudents should be able to describe the nature of the compounds\nformed when chlorine, bromine and iodine react with metals and\nnon-metals.\nIn Group 7, the further down the group an element is the higher its\nrelative molecular mass, melting point and boiling point.\nIn Group 7, the reactivity of the elements decreases going down the\ngroup.\nA more reactive halogen can displace a less reactive halogen from\nan aqueous solution of its salt.\nAT 6\nOffers an opportunity within\ndisplacement reactions of\nhalogens.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how properties of the elements in Group 7 depend on\nthe outer shell of electrons of the atoms\n• predict properties from given trends down the group.\nWS 1.2\n4.1.3 Properties of transition metals (chemistry only)\n4.1.3.1 Comparison with Group 1 elements\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe transition elements are metals with similar properties which are\ndifferent from those of the elements in Group 1.\nStudents should be able to describe the difference compared with\nGroup 1 in melting points, densities, strength, hardness and\nreactivity with oxygen, water and halogens.\nStudents should be able to exemplify these general properties by\nreference to Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 25\n4.1.3.2 Typical properties\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMany transition elements have ions with different charges, form\ncoloured compounds and are useful as catalysts.\nStudents should be able to exemplify these general properties by\nreference to compounds of Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu.\n4.2 Bonding, structure, and the properties of matter\nChemists use theories of structure and bonding to explain the physical and chemical properties of\nmaterials. Analysis of structures shows that atoms can be arranged in a variety of ways, some of\nwhich are molecular while others are giant structures. Theories of bonding explain how atoms are\nheld together in these structures. Scientists use this knowledge of structure and bonding to\nengineer new materials with desirable properties. The properties of these materials may offer new\napplications in a range of different technologies.\n4.2.1 Chemical bonds, ionic, covalent and metallic\n4.2.1.1 Chemical bonds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThere are three types of strong chemical bonds: ionic, covalent and\nmetallic. For ionic bonding the particles are oppositely charged ions.\nFor covalent bonding the particles are atoms which share pairs of\nelectrons. For metallic bonding the particles are atoms which share\ndelocalised electrons.\nIonic bonding occurs in compounds formed from metals combined\nwith non-metals.\nCovalent bonding occurs in most non-metallic elements and in\ncompounds of non-metals.\nMetallic bonding occurs in metallic elements and alloys.\nStudents should be able to explain chemical bonding in terms of\nelectrostatic forces and the transfer or sharing of electrons.\n26 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration"

          "id": "aqa-gcse-chemistry-8462-v1.1:4-2",
          "code": "4.2",
          "title": "Bonding, structure, and the properties of matter",
          "kind": "chapter",
          "tier": [
            "Foundation",
            "Higher"

          "locator": "Official PDF pages 26-36; 4.2 Bonding, structure, and the properties of matter",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 26-36\nBonding, structure, and the properties of matter\nChemists use theories of structure and bonding to explain the physical and chemical properties of\nmaterials. Analysis of structures shows that atoms can be arranged in a variety of ways, some of\nwhich are molecular while others are giant structures. Theories of bonding explain how atoms are\nheld together in these structures. Scientists use this knowledge of structure and bonding to\nengineer new materials with desirable properties. The properties of these materials may offer new\napplications in a range of different technologies.\n4.2.1 Chemical bonds, ionic, covalent and metallic\n4.2.1.1 Chemical bonds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThere are three types of strong chemical bonds: ionic, covalent and\nmetallic. For ionic bonding the particles are oppositely charged ions.\nFor covalent bonding the particles are atoms which share pairs of\nelectrons. For metallic bonding the particles are atoms which share\ndelocalised electrons.\nIonic bonding occurs in compounds formed from metals combined\nwith non-metals.\nCovalent bonding occurs in most non-metallic elements and in\ncompounds of non-metals.\nMetallic bonding occurs in metallic elements and alloys.\nStudents should be able to explain chemical bonding in terms of\nelectrostatic forces and the transfer or sharing of electrons.\n26 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.1.2 Ionic bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhen a metal atom reacts with a non-metal atom electrons in the\nouter shell of the metal atom are transferred. Metal atoms lose\nelectrons to become positively charged ions. Non-metal atoms gain\nelectrons to become negatively charged ions. The ions produced by\nmetals in Groups 1 and 2 and by non-metals in Groups 6 and 7\nhave the electronic structure of a noble gas (Group 0).\nThe electron transfer during the formation of an ionic compound can\nbe represented by a dot and cross diagram, eg for sodium chloride\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to draw dot and cross diagrams for ionic\ncompounds formed by metals in Groups 1 and 2 with non-metals in\nGroups 6 and 7.\nThe charge on the ions produced by metals in Groups 1 and 2 and\nby non-metals in Groups 6 and 7 relates to the group number of the\nelement in the periodic table.\nStudents should be able to work out the charge on the ions of\nmetals and non-metals from the group number of the element,\nlimited to the metals in Groups 1 and 2, and non-metals in Groups 6\nand 7.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.1.3 Ionic compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAn ionic compound is a giant structure of ions. Ionic compounds are\nheld together by strong electrostatic forces of attraction between\noppositely charged ions. These forces act in all directions in the\nlattice and this is called ionic bonding.\nThe structure of sodium chloride can be represented in the following\nforms:\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 27\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to:\n• deduce that a compound is ionic from a diagram of its\nstructure in one of the specified forms\n• describe the limitations of using dot and cross, ball and stick,\ntwo and three-dimensional diagrams to represent a giant ionic\nstructure\n• work out the empirical formula of an ionic compound from a\ngiven model or diagram that shows the ions in the structure.\nStudents should be familiar with the structure of sodium chloride but\ndo not need to know the structures of other ionic compounds.\nWS 1.2\nMS 4a\nMS 1a, 1c\n28 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.1.4 Covalent bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhen atoms share pairs of electrons, they form covalent bonds.\nThese bonds between atoms are strong.\nCovalently bonded substances may consist of small molecules.\nStudents should be able to recognise common substances that\nconsist of small molecules from their chemical formula.\nSome covalently bonded substances have very large molecules,\nsuch as polymers.\nSome covalently bonded substances have giant covalent\nstructures, such as diamond and silicon dioxide.\nThe covalent bonds in molecules and giant structures can be\nrepresented in the following forms:\nPolymers can be represented in the form:\nwhere n is a large number.\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances as\nsmall molecules, polymers\nor giant structures from\ndiagrams showing their\nbonding.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 29\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to:\n• draw dot and cross diagrams for the molecules of hydrogen,\nchlorine, oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen chloride, water,\nammonia and methane\n• represent the covalent bonds in small molecules, in the\nrepeating units of polymers and in part of giant covalent\nstructures, using a line to represent a single bond\n• describe the limitations of using dot and cross, ball and stick,\ntwo and three-dimensional diagrams to represent molecules\nor giant structures\n• deduce the molecular formula of a substance from a given\nmodel or diagram in these forms showing the atoms and\nbonds in the molecule.\nMS 5b\n4.2.1.5 Metallic bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals consist of giant structures of atoms arranged in a regular\npattern.\nThe electrons in the outer shell of metal atoms are delocalised and\nso are free to move through the whole structure. The sharing of\ndelocalised electrons gives rise to strong metallic bonds. The\nbonding in metals may be represented in the following form:\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances as\nmetallic giant structures\nfrom diagrams showing\ntheir bonding.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n30 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.2 How bonding and structure are related to the properties of\nsubstances\n4.2.2.1 The three states of matter\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe three states of matter are solid, liquid and gas. Melting and\nfreezing take place at the melting point, boiling and condensing\ntake place at the boiling point.\nThe three states of matter can be represented by a simple model. In\nthis model, particles are represented by small solid spheres.\nParticle theory can help to explain melting, boiling, freezing and\ncondensing.\nThe amount of energy needed to change state from solid to liquid\nand from liq"

Melting and\nfreezing take place at the melting point, boiling and condensing\ntake place at the boiling point.\nThe three states of matter can be represented by a simple model. In\nthis model, particles are represented by small solid spheres.\nParticle theory can help to explain melting, boiling, freezing and\ncondensing.\nThe amount of energy needed to change state from solid to liquid\nand from liquid to gas depends on the strength of the forces\nbetween the particles of the substance. The nature of the particles\ninvolved depends on the type of bonding and the structure of the\nsubstance. The stronger the forces between the particles the higher\nthe melting point and boiling point of the substance.\n(HT only) Limitations of the simple model above include that in the\nmodel there are no forces, that all particles are represented as\nspheres and that the spheres are solid.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents"

Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 27\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to:\n• deduce that a compound is ionic from a diagram of its\nstructure in one of the specified forms\n• describe the limitations of using dot and cross, ball and stick,\ntwo and three-dimensional diagrams to represent a giant ionic\nstructure\n• work out the empirical formula of an ionic compound from a\ngiven model or diagram that shows the ions in the structure.\nStudents should be familiar with the structure of sodium chloride but\ndo not need to know the structures of other ionic compounds.\nWS 1.2\nMS 4a\nMS 1a, 1c\n28 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.1.4 Covalent bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhen atoms share pairs of electrons, they form covalent bonds.\nThese bonds between atoms are strong.\nCovalently bonded substances may consist of small molecules.\nStudents should be able to recognise common substances that\nconsist of small molecules from their chemical formula.\nSome covalently bonded substances have very large molecules,\nsuch as polymers.\nSome covalently bonded substances have giant covalent\nstructures, such as diamond and silicon dioxide.\nThe covalent bonds in molecules and giant structures can be\nrepresented in the following forms:\nPolymers can be represented in the form:\nwhere n is a large number.\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances as\nsmall molecules, polymers\nor giant structures from\ndiagrams showing their\nbonding.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 29\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to:\n• draw dot and cross diagrams for the molecules of hydrogen,\nchlorine, oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen chloride, water,\nammonia and methane\n• represent the covalent bonds in small molecules, in the\nrepeating units of polymers and in part of giant covalent\nstructures, using a line to represent a single bond\n• describe the limitations of using dot and cross, ball and stick,\ntwo and three-dimensional diagrams to represent molecules\nor giant structures\n• deduce the molecular formula of a substance from a given\nmodel or diagram in these forms showing the atoms and\nbonds in the molecule.\nMS 5b\n4.2.1.5 Metallic bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals consist of giant structures of atoms arranged in a regular\npattern.\nThe electrons in the outer shell of metal atoms are delocalised and\nso are free to move through the whole structure. The sharing of\ndelocalised electrons gives rise to strong metallic bonds. The\nbonding in metals may be represented in the following form:\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances as\nmetallic giant structures\nfrom diagrams showing\ntheir bonding.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n30 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.2 How bonding and structure are related to the properties of\nsubstances\n4.2.2.1 The three states of matter\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe three states of matter are solid, liquid and gas. Melting and\nfreezing take place at the melting point, boiling and condensing\ntake place at the boiling point.\nThe three states of matter can be represented by a simple model. In\nthis model, particles are represented by small solid spheres.\nParticle theory can help to explain melting, boiling, freezing and\ncondensing.\nThe amount of energy needed to change state from solid to liquid\nand from liquid to gas depends on the strength of the forces\nbetween the particles of the substance. The nature of the particles\ninvolved depends on the type of bonding and the structure of the\nsubstance. The stronger the forces between the particles the higher\nthe melting point and boiling point of the substance.\n(HT only) Limitations of the simple model above include that in the\nmodel there are no forces, that all particles are represented as\nspheres and that the spheres are solid.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to:\n• predict the states of substances at different temperatures\ngiven appropriate data\n• explain the different temperatures at which changes of state\noccur in terms of energy transfers and types of bonding\n• recognise that atoms themselves do not have the bulk\nproperties of materials\n• (HT only) explain the limitations of the particle theory in\nrelation to changes of state when particles are represented by\nsolid inelastic spheres which have no forces between them.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 31\n4.2.2.2 State symbols\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn chemical equations, the three states of matter are shown as (s),\n(l) and (g), with (aq) for aqueous solutions.\nStudents should be able to include appropriate state symbols in\nchemical equations for the reactions in this specification.\n4.2.2.3 Properties of ionic compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIonic compounds have regular structures (giant ionic lattices) in\nwhich there are strong electrostatic forces of attraction in all\ndirections between oppositely charged ions.\nThese compounds have high melting points and high boiling points\nbecause of the large amounts of energy needed to break the many\nstrong bonds.\nWhen melted or dissolved in water, ionic compounds conduct\nelectricity because the ions are free to move and so charge can\nflow.\nKnowledge of the structures of specific ionic compounds other than\nsodium chloride is not required.\n4.2.2.4 Properties of small molecules\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSubstances that consist of small molecules are usually gases or\nliquids that have relatively low melting points and boiling points.\nThese substances have only weak forces between the molecules\n(intermolecular forces). It is these intermolecular forces that are\novercome, not the covalent bonds, when the substance melts or\nboils.\nThe intermolecular forces incre"

For metallic bonding the particles are atoms which share\ndelocalised electrons.\nIonic bonding occurs in compounds formed from metals combined\nwith non-metals.\nCovalent bonding occurs in most non-metallic elements and in\ncompounds of non-metals.\nMetallic bonding occurs in metallic elements and alloys.\nStudents should be able to explain chemical bonding in terms of\nelectrostatic forces and the transfer or sharing of electrons.\n26 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.1.2 Ionic bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhen a metal atom reacts with a non-metal atom electrons in the\nouter shell of the metal atom are transferred. Metal atoms lose\nelectrons to become positively charged ions. Non-metal atoms gain\nelectrons to become negatively charged ions. The ions produced by\nmetals in Groups 1 and 2 and by non-metals in Groups 6 and 7\nhave the electronic structure of a noble gas (Group 0).\nThe electron transfer during the formation of an ionic compound can\nbe represented by a dot and cross diagram, eg for sodium chloride\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to draw dot and cross diagrams for ionic\ncompounds formed by metals in Groups 1 and 2 with non-metals in\nGroups 6 and 7.\nThe charge on the ions produced by metals in Groups 1 and 2 and\nby non-metals in Groups 6 and 7 relates to the group number of the\nelement in the periodic table.\nStudents should be able to work out the charge on the ions of\nmetals and non-metals from the group number of the element,\nlimited to the metals in Groups 1 and 2, and non-metals in Groups 6\nand 7.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.1.3 Ionic compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAn ionic compound is a giant structure of ions.           "text": "Official PDF pages 26-36\n4.1.3.2 Typical properties\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMany transition elements have ions with different charges, form\ncoloured compounds and are useful as catalysts.\nStudents should be able to exemplify these general properties by\nreference to compounds of Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu.\n4.2 Bonding, structure, and the properties of matter\nChemists use theories of structure and bonding to explain the physical and chemical properties of\nmaterials. Analysis of structures shows that atoms can be arranged in a variety of ways, some of\nwhich are molecular while others are giant structures. Theories of bonding explain how atoms are\nheld together in these structures. Scientists use this knowledge of structure and bonding to\nengineer new materials with desirable properties. The properties of these materials may offer new\napplications in a range of different technologies.\n4.2.1 Chemical bonds, ionic, covalent and metallic\n4.2.1.1 Chemical bonds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThere are three types of strong chemical bonds: ionic, covalent and\nmetallic. For ionic bonding the particles are oppositely charged ions.\nFor covalent bonding the particles are atoms which share pairs of\nelectrons. For metallic bonding the particles are atoms which share\ndelocalised electrons.\nIonic bonding occurs in compounds formed from metals combined\nwith non-metals.\nCovalent bonding occurs in most non-metallic elements and in\ncompounds of non-metals.\nMetallic bonding occurs in metallic elements and alloys.\nStudents should be able to explain chemical bonding in terms of\nelectrostatic forces and the transfer or sharing of electrons.\n26 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.1.2 Ionic bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhen a metal atom reacts with a non-metal atom electrons in the\nouter shell of the metal atom are transferred. Metal atoms lose\nelectrons to become positively charged ions. Non-metal atoms gain\nelectrons to become negatively charged ions. The ions produced by\nmetals in Groups 1 and 2 and by non-metals in Groups 6 and 7\nhave the electronic structure of a noble gas (Group 0).\nThe electron transfer during the formation of an ionic compound can\nbe represented by a dot and cross diagram, eg for sodium chloride\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to draw dot and cross diagrams for ionic\ncompounds formed by metals in Groups 1 and 2 with non-metals in\nGroups 6 and 7.\nThe charge on the ions produced by metals in Groups 1 and 2 and\nby non-metals in Groups 6 and 7 relates to the group number of the\nelement in the periodic table.\nStudents should be able to work out the charge on the ions of\nmetals and non-metals from the group number of the element,\nlimited to the metals in Groups 1 and 2, and non-metals in Groups 6\nand 7.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.1.3 Ionic compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAn ionic compound is a giant structure of ions. Ionic compounds are\nheld together by strong electrostatic forces of attraction between\noppositely charged ions. These forces act in all directions in the\nlattice and this is called ionic bonding.\nThe structure of sodium chloride can be represented in the following\nforms:\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. The stronger the forces between the particles the higher\nthe melting point and boiling point of the substance.\n(HT only) Limitations of the simple model above include that in the\nmodel there are no forces, that all particles are represented as\nspheres and that the spheres are solid.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to:\n• predict the states of substances at different temperatures\ngiven appropriate data\n• explain the different temperatures at which changes of state\noccur in terms of energy transfers and types of bonding\n• recognise that atoms themselves do not have the bulk\nproperties of materials\n• (HT only) explain the limitations of the particle theory in\nrelation to changes of state when particles are represented by\nsolid inelastic spheres which have no forces between them.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 31\n4.2.2.2 State symbols\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn chemical equations, the three states of matter are shown as (s),\n(l) and (g), with (aq) for aqueous solutions.\nStudents should be able to include appropriate state symbols in\nchemical equations for the reactions in this specification.\n4.2.2.3 Properties of ionic compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIonic compounds have regular structures (giant ionic lattices) in\nwhich there are strong electrostatic forces of attraction in all\ndirections between oppositely charged ions.\nThese compounds have high melting points and high boiling points\nbecause of the large amounts of energy needed to break the many\nstrong bonds.\nWhen melted or dissolved in water, ionic compounds conduct\nelectricity because the ions are free to move and so charge can\nflow.\nKnowledge of the structures of specific ionic compounds other than\nsodium chloride is not required.\n4.2.2.4 Properties of small molecules\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSubstances that consist of small molecules are usually gases or\nliquids that have relatively low melting points and boiling points.\nThese substances have only weak forces between the molecules\n(intermolecular forces). It is these intermolecular forces that are\novercome, not the covalent bonds, when the substance melts or\nboils.\nThe intermolecular forces increase with the size of the molecules,\nso larger molecules have higher melting and boiling points.\nThese substances do not conduct electricity because the molecules\ndo not have an overall electric charge.\nStudents should be able to use the idea that intermolecular forces\nare weak compared with covalent bonds to explain the bulk\nproperties of molecular substances.\nWS 1.2\n32 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.2.5 Polymers\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nPolymers have very large molecules. The atoms in the polymer\nmolecules are linked to other atoms by strong covalent bonds. The\nintermolecular forces between polymer molecules are relatively\nstrong and so these substances are solids at room temperature.\nStudents should be able to recognise polymers from diagrams\nshowing their bonding and structure.\n4.2.2.6 Giant covalent structures\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSubstances that consist of giant covalent structures are solids with\nvery high melting points. All of the atoms in these structures are\nlinked to other atoms by strong covalent bonds. These bonds must\nbe overcome to melt or boil these substances. Diamond and\ngraphite (forms of carbon) and silicon dioxide (silica) are examples\nof giant covalent structures.\nStudents should be able to recognise giant covalent structures from\ndiagrams showing their bonding and structure.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.2.7 Properties of metals and alloys\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals have giant structures of atoms with strong metallic bonding.\nThis means that most metals have high melting and boiling points.\nIn pure metals, atoms are arranged in layers, which allows metals\nto be bent and shaped. Pure metals are too soft for many uses and\nso are mixed with other metals to make alloys which are harder.\nStudents should be able to explain why alloys are harder than pure\nmetals in terms of distortion of the layers of atoms in the structure of\na pure metal.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.2.8 Metals as conductors\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals are good conductors of electricity because the delocalised\nelectrons in the metal carry electrical charge through the metal.\nMetals are good conductors of thermal energy because energy is\ntransferred by the delocalised electrons.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 33\n4.2.3 Structure and bonding of carbon\n4.2.3.1 Diamond\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn diamond, each carbon atom forms four covalent bonds with other\ncarbon atoms in a giant covalent structure, so diamond is very hard,\nhas a very high melting point and does not conduct electricity.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to explain the properties of diamond in\nterms of its structure and bonding.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.3.2 Graphite\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn graphite, each carbon atom forms three covalent bonds with\nthree other carbon atoms, forming layers of hexagonal rings which\nhave no covalent bonds between the layers.\nIn graphite, one electron from each carbon atom is delocalised.\nStudents should be able to explain the properties of graphite in\nterms of its structure and bonding.\nStudents should know that graphite is similar to metals in that it has\ndelocalised electrons.\nWS 1.2\n34 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.3.3 Graphene and fullerenes\nContent Key opportunities for\nsk"
