Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 29\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to:\n• draw dot and cross diagrams for the molecules of hydrogen,\nchlorine, oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen chloride, water,\nammonia and methane\n• represent the covalent bonds in small molecules, in the\nrepeating units of polymers and in part of giant covalent\nstructures, using a line to represent a single bond\n• describe the limitations of using dot and cross, ball and stick,\ntwo and three-dimensional diagrams to represent molecules\nor giant structures\n• deduce the molecular formula of a substance from a given\nmodel or diagram in these forms showing the atoms and\nbonds in the molecule.\nMS 5b\n4.2.1.5 Metallic bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals consist of giant structures of atoms arranged in a regular\npattern.\nThe electrons in the outer shell of metal atoms are delocalised and\nso are free to move through the whole structure. The sharing of\ndelocalised electrons gives rise to strong metallic bonds. The\nbonding in metals may be represented in the following form:\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances as\nmetallic giant structures\nfrom diagrams showing\ntheir bonding.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n30 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.2 How bonding and structure are related to the properties of\nsubstances\n4.2.2.1 The three states of matter\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe three states of matter are solid, liquid and gas. Melting and\nfreezing take place at the melting point, boiling and condensing\ntake place at the boiling point.\nThe three states of matter can be represented by a simple model. In\nthis model, particles are represented by small solid spheres.\nParticle theory can help to explain melting, boiling, freezing and\ncondensing.\nThe amount of energy needed to change state from solid to liquid\nand from liquid to gas depends on the strength of the forces\nbetween the particles of the substance. The nature of the particles\ninvolved depends on the type of bonding and the structure of the\nsubstance. The stronger the forces between the particles the higher\nthe melting point and boiling point of the substance.\n(HT only) Limitations of the simple model above include that in the\nmodel there are no forces, that all particles are represented as\nspheres and that the spheres are solid.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to:\n• predict the states of substances at different temperatures\ngiven appropriate data\n• explain the different temperatures at which changes of state\noccur in terms of energy transfers and types of bonding\n• recognise that atoms themselves do not have the bulk\nproperties of materials\n• (HT only) explain the limitations of the particle theory in\nrelation to changes of state when particles are represented by\nsolid inelastic spheres which have no forces between them.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 31\n4.2.2.2 State symbols\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn chemical equations, the three states of matter are shown as (s),\n(l) and (g), with (aq) for aqueous solutions.\nStudents should be able to include appropriate state symbols in\nchemical equations for the reactions in this specification.\n4.2.2.3 Properties of ionic compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIonic compounds have regular structures (giant ionic lattices) in\nwhich there are strong electrostatic forces of attraction in all\ndirections between oppositely charged ions.\nThese compounds have high melting points and high boiling points\nbecause of the large amounts of energy needed to break the many\nstrong bonds.\nWhen melted or dissolved in water, ionic compounds conduct\nelectricity because the ions are free to move and so charge can\nflow.\nKnowledge of the structures of specific ionic compounds other than\nsodium chloride is not required.\n4.2.2.4 Properties of small molecules\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSubstances that consist of small molecules are usually gases or\nliquids that have relatively low melting points and boiling points.\nThese substances have only weak forces between the molecules\n(intermolecular forces). It is these intermolecular forces that are\novercome, not the covalent bonds, when the substance melts or\nboils.\nThe intermolecular forces increase with the size of the molecules,\nso larger molecules have higher melting and boiling points.\nThese substances do not conduct electricity because the molecules\ndo not have an overall electric charge.\nStudents should be able to use the idea that intermolecular forces\nare weak compared with covalent bonds to explain the bulk\nproperties of molecular substances.\nWS 1.2\n32 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.2.5 Polymers\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nPolymers have very large molecules. The atoms in the polymer\nmolecules are linked to other atoms by strong covalent bonds. The\nintermolecular forces between polymer molecules are relatively\nstrong and so these substances are solids at room temperature.\nStudents should be able to recognise polymers from diagrams\nshowing their bonding and structure.\n4.2.2.6 Giant covalent structures\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSubstances that consist of giant covalent structures are solids with\nvery high melting points. All of the atoms in these structures are\nlinked to other atoms by strong covalent bonds. These bonds must\nbe overcome to melt or boil these substances. Diamond and\ngraphite (forms of carbon) and silicon dioxide (silica) are examples\nof giant covalent structures.\nStudents should be able to recognise giant covalent structures from\ndiagrams showing their bonding and structure.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.2.7 Properties of metals and alloys\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals have giant structures of atoms with strong metallic bonding.\nThis means that most metals have high melting and boiling points.\nIn pure"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 26-36\nGiant covalent structures\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSubstances that consist of giant covalent structures are solids with\nvery high melting points. All of the atoms in these structures are\nlinked to other atoms by strong covalent bonds. These bonds must\nbe overcome to melt or boil these substances. Diamond and\ngraphite (forms of carbon) and silicon dioxide (silica) are examples\nof giant covalent structures.\nStudents should be able to recognise giant covalent structures from\ndiagrams showing their bonding and structure.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.2.7 Properties of metals and alloys\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals have giant structures of atoms with strong metallic bonding.\nThis means that most metals have high melting and boiling points.\nIn pure metals, atoms are arranged in layers, which allows metals\nto be bent and shaped. Pure metals are too soft for many uses and\nso are mixed with other metals to make alloys which are harder.\nStudents should be able to explain why alloys are harder than pure\nmetals in terms of distortion of the layers of atoms in the structure of\na pure metal.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.2.8 Metals as conductors\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals are good conductors of electricity because the delocalised\nelectrons in the metal carry electrical charge through the metal.\nMetals are good conductors of thermal energy because energy is\ntransferred by the delocalised electrons.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 33\n4.2.3 Structure and bonding of carbon\n4.2.3.1 Diamond\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn diamond, each carbon atom forms four covalent bonds with other\ncarbon atoms in a giant covalent structure, so diamond is very hard,\nhas a very high melting point and does not conduct electricity.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to explain the properties of diamond in\nterms of its structure and bonding.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.3.2 Graphite\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn graphite, each carbon atom forms three covalent bonds with\nthree other carbon atoms, forming layers of hexagonal rings which\nhave no covalent bonds between the layers.\nIn graphite, one electron from each carbon atom is delocalised.\nStudents should be able to explain the properties of graphite in\nterms of its structure and bonding.\nStudents should know that graphite is similar to metals in that it has\ndelocalised electrons.\nWS 1.2\n34 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.3.3 Graphene and fullerenes\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nGraphene is a single layer of graphite and has properties that make\nit useful in electronics and composites.\nStudents should be able to explain the properties of graphene in\nterms of its structure and bonding.\nFullerenes are molecules of carbon atoms with hollow shapes. The\nstructure of fullerenes is based on hexagonal rings of carbon atoms\nbut they may also contain rings with five or seven carbon atoms.\nThe first fullerene to be discovered was Buckminsterfullerene (C60)\nwhich has a spherical shape.\nCarbon nanotubes are cylindrical fullerenes with very high length to\ndiameter ratios. Their properties make them useful for\nnanotechnology, electronics and materials.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recognise graphene and fullerenes from diagrams and\ndescriptions of their bonding and structure\n• give examples of the uses of fullerenes, including carbon\nnanotubes.\nWS 1.2, 1.4\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n4.2.4 Bulk and surface properties of matter including nanoparticles\n(chemistry only)\n4.2.4.1 Sizes of particles and their properties\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNanoscience refers to structures that are 1–100 nm in size, of the\norder of a few hundred atoms. Nanoparticles, are smaller than fine\nparticles (PM2.5), which have diameters between 100 and 2500 nm\n(1 x 10-7 m and 2.5 x 10-6 m). Coarse particles (PM10) have\ndiameters between 1 x 10-5 m and 2.5 x 10-6 m. Coarse particles\nare often referred to as dust.\nAs the side of cube decreases by a factor of 10 the surface area to\nvolume ratio increases by a factor of 10.\nNanoparticles may have properties different from those for the\nsame materials in bulk because of their high surface area to\nvolume ratio. It may also mean that smaller quantities are needed\nto be effective than for materials with normal particle sizes.\nWS 1.2, 1.4, 4.1, 4.2, 4.3\n4.4, 4.5\nMS 2h\nMake order of magnitude\ncalculations.\nMS 5c\nCalculate areas of triangles\nand rectangles, surface\nareas and volumes of cubes.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 35\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to compare ‘nano’ dimensions to typical\ndimensions of atoms and molecules.\nMS 1b\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in standard\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\n4.2.4.2 Uses of nanoparticles\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNanoparticles have many applications in medicine, in electronics, in\ncosmetics and sun creams, as deodorants, and as catalysts. New\napplications for nanoparticulate materials are an important area of\nresearch.\nStudents should consider advantages and disadvantages of the\napplications of these nanoparticulate materials, but do not need to\nknow specific examples or properties other than those specified.\nStudents should be able to:\n• given appropriate information, evaluate the use of\nnanoparticles for a specified purpose\n• explain that there are possible risks associated with the use of\nnanoparticles.\nWS 1.3, 1.4, 1.5\n4.3 Quantitative chemistry\nChemists use quantitative analysis to determine the formulae of compounds and the equations for\nreactions. Given this information, analysts can then use quantitative methods to determine the\npurity of chemical samples and to monitor the yield from chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be classified in various ways. Identifying different types of chemical\nreaction allows chemists to make sense of how different chemicals react together, to establish\npatterns and to make predictions about the behaviour of other chemicals. Chemical equations\nprovide a means of representing chemical reactions and are a key way for chemists to\ncommunicate chemical ideas.\n36 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration"

GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 29\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to:\n• draw dot and cross diagrams for the molecules of hydrogen,\nchlorine, oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen chloride, water,\nammonia and methane\n• represent the covalent bonds in small molecules, in the\nrepeating units of polymers and in part of giant covalent\nstructures, using a line to represent a single bond\n• describe the limitations of using dot and cross, ball and stick,\ntwo and three-dimensional diagrams to represent molecules\nor giant structures\n• deduce the molecular formula of a substance from a given\nmodel or diagram in these forms showing the atoms and\nbonds in the molecule.\nMS 5b\n4.2.1.5 Metallic bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals consist of giant structures of atoms arranged in a regular\npattern.\nThe electrons in the outer shell of metal atoms are delocalised and\nso are free to move through the whole structure. The sharing of\ndelocalised electrons gives rise to strong metallic bonds. The\nbonding in metals may be represented in the following form:\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances as\nmetallic giant structures\nfrom diagrams showing\ntheir bonding.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n30 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.2 How bonding and structure are related to the properties of\nsubstances\n4.2.2.1 The three states of matter\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe three states of matter are solid, liquid and gas. Melting and\nfreezing take place at the melting point, boiling and condensing\ntake place at the boiling point.\nThe three states of matter can be represented by a simple model. In\nthis model, particles are represented by small solid spheres.\nParticle theory can help to explain melting, boiling, freezing and\ncondensing.\nThe amount of energy needed to change state from solid to liquid\nand from liquid to gas depends on the strength of the forces\nbetween the particles of the substance. The nature of the particles\ninvolved depends on the type of bonding and the structure of the\nsubstance. The stronger the forces between the particles the higher\nthe melting point and boiling point of the substance.\n(HT only) Limitations of the simple model above include that in the\nmodel there are no forces, that all particles are represented as\nspheres and that the spheres are solid.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to:\n• predict the states of substances at different temperatures\ngiven appropriate data\n• explain the different temperatures at which changes of state\noccur in terms of energy transfers and types of bonding\n• recognise that atoms themselves do not have the bulk\nproperties of materials\n• (HT only) explain the limitations of the particle theory in\nrelation to changes of state when particles are represented by\nsolid inelastic spheres which have no forces between them.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 31\n4.2.2.2 State symbols\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn chemical equations, the three states of matter are shown as (s),\n(l) and (g), with (aq) for aqueous solutions.\nStudents should be able to include appropriate state symbols in\nchemical equations for the reactions in this specification.\n4.2.2.3 Properties of ionic compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIonic compounds have regular structures (giant ionic lattices) in\nwhich there are strong electrostatic forces of attraction in all\ndirections between oppositely charged ions.\nThese compounds have high melting points and high boiling points\nbecause of the large amounts of energy needed to break the many\nstrong bonds.\nWhen melted or dissolved in water, ionic compounds conduct\nelectricity because the ions are free to move and so charge can\nflow.\nKnowledge of the structures of specific ionic compounds other than\nsodium chloride is not required.\n4.2.2.4 Properties of small molecules\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSubstances that consist of small molecules are usually gases or\nliquids that have relatively low melting points and boiling points.\nThese substances have only weak forces between the molecules\n(intermolecular forces). It is these intermolecular forces that are\novercome, not the covalent bonds, when the substance melts or\nboils.\nThe intermolecular forces increase with the size of the molecules,\nso larger molecules have higher melting and boiling points.\nThese substances do not conduct electricity because the molecules\ndo not have an overall electric charge.\nStudents should be able to use the idea that intermolecular forces\nare weak compared with covalent bonds to explain the bulk\nproperties of molecular substances.\nWS 1.2\n32 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.2.5 Polymers\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nPolymers have very large molecules. The atoms in the polymer\nmolecules are linked to other atoms by strong covalent bonds. The\nintermolecular forces between polymer molecules are relatively\nstrong and so these substances are solids at room temperature.\nStudents should be able to recognise polymers from diagrams\nshowing their bonding and structure.\n4.2.2.6 Giant covalent structures\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSubstances that consist of giant covalent structures are solids with\nvery high melting points. All of the atoms in these structures are\nlinked to other atoms by strong covalent bonds. These bonds must\nbe overcome to melt or boil these substances. Diamond and\ngraphite (forms of carbon) and silicon dioxide (silica) are examples\nof giant covalent structures.\nStudents should be able to recognise giant covalent structures from\ndiagrams showing their bonding and structure.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.2.7 Properties of metals and alloys\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals have giant structures of atoms with strong metallic bonding.\nThis means that most metals have high melting and boiling points.\nIn pure metals, atoms are arranged in layers, which allows metals\nto be bent and shaped. Pure metals are too soft for many uses and\nso are mixed with other metals to make alloys which are harder.\nStudents should be able to explain why alloys are harder than pure\nmetals in terms of distortion of the layers of atoms in the structure of\na pure metal.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.2.8 Metals as conductors\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals are good conductors of electricity because the delocalised\nelectrons in the metal carry electrical charge through the metal.\nMetals are good conductors of thermal energy because energy is\ntransferred by the delocalised electrons.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 33\n4.2.3 Structure and bonding of carbon\n4.2.3.1 Diamond\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn diamond, each carbon atom forms four covalent bonds with other\ncarbon atoms in a giant covalent structure, so diamond is very hard,\nhas a very high melting point and does not conduct electricity.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to explain the properties of diamond in\nterms of its structure and bonding.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.3.2 Graphite\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn graphite, each carbon atom forms three covalent bonds with\nthree other carbon atoms, forming layers of hexagonal rings which\nhave no covalent bonds between the layers.\nIn graphite, one electron from each carbon atom is delocalised.\nStudents should be able to explain the properties of graphite in\nterms of its structure and bonding.\nStudents should know that graphite is similar to metals in that it has\ndelocalised electrons.\nWS 1.2\n34 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.3.3 Graphene and fullerenes\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nGraphene is a single layer of graphite and has properties that make\nit useful in electronics and composites.\nStudents should be able to explain the properties of graphene in\nterms of its structure and bonding.\nFullerenes are molecules of carbon atoms with hollow shapes. The\nstructure of fullerenes is based on hexagonal rings of carbon atoms\nbut they may also contain rings with five or seven carbon atoms.\nThe first fullerene to be discovered was Buckminsterfullerene (C60)\nwhich has a spherical shape.\nCarbon nanotubes are cylindrical fullerenes with very high length to\ndiameter ratios. Their properties make them useful for\nnanotechnology, electronics and materials.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recognise graphene and fullerenes from diagrams and\ndescriptions of their bonding and structure\n• give examples of the uses of fullerenes, including carbon\nnanotubes.\nWS 1.2, 1.4\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n4.2.4 Bulk and surface properties of matter including nanoparticles\n(chemistry only)\n4.2.4.1 Sizes of particles and their properties\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNanoscience refers to structures that are 1–100 nm in size, of the\norder of a few hundred atoms. Nanoparticles, are smaller than fine\nparticles (PM2.5), which have diameters between 100 and 2500 nm\n(1 x 10-7 m and 2.5 x 10-6 m). Coarse particles (PM10) have\ndiameters between 1 x 10-5 m and 2.5 x 10-6 m. Coarse particles\nare often referred to as dust.\nAs the side of cube decreases by a factor of 10 the surface area to\nvolume ratio increases by a factor of 10.\nNanoparticles may have properties different from those for the\nsame materials in bulk because of their high surface area to\nvolume ratio. It may also mean that smaller quantities are needed\nto be effective than for materials with normal particle sizes.\nWS 1.2, 1.4, 4.1, 4.2, 4.3\n4.4, 4.5\nMS 2h\nMake order of magnitude\ncalculations.\nMS 5c\nCalculate areas of triangles\nand rectangles, surface\nareas and volumes of cubes.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 35\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to compare ‘nano’ dimensions to typical\ndimensions of atoms and molecules.\nMS 1b\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in standard\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\n4.2.4.2 Uses of nanoparticles\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNanoparticles have many applications in medicine, in electronics, in\ncosmetics and sun creams, as deodorants, and as catalysts. New\napplications for nanoparticulate materials are an important area of\nresearch.\nStudents should consider advantages and disadvantages of the\napplications of these nanoparticulate materials, but do not need to\nknow specific examples or properties other than those specified.\nStudents should be able to:\n• given appropriate information, evaluate the use of\nnanoparticles for a specified purpose\n• explain that there are possible risks associated with the use of\nnanoparticles.\nWS 1.3, 1.4, 1.5\n4.3 Quantitative chemistry\nChemists use quantitative analysis to determine the formulae of compounds and the equations for\nreactions. Given this information, analysts can then use quantitative methods to determine the\npurity of chemical samples and to monitor the yield from chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be classified in various ways. Identifying different types of chemical\nreaction allows chemists to make sense of how different chemicals react together, to establish\npatterns and to make predictions about the behaviour of other chemicals. Chemical equations\nprovide a means of representing chemical reactions and are a key way for chemists to\ncommunicate chemical ideas.\n36 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 26-36\n4.1.3.2 Typical properties\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMany transition elements have ions with different charges, form\ncoloured compounds and are useful as catalysts.\nStudents should be able to exemplify these general properties by\nreference to compounds of Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu.\n4.2 Bonding, structure, and the properties of matter\nChemists use theories of structure and bonding to explain the physical and chemical properties of\nmaterials. Analysis of structures shows that atoms can be arranged in a variety of ways, some of\nwhich are molecular while others are giant structures. Theories of bonding explain how atoms are\nheld together in these structures. Scientists use this knowledge of structure and bonding to\nengineer new materials with desirable properties. The properties of these materials may offer new\napplications in a range of different technologies.\n4.2.1 Chemical bonds, ionic, covalent and metallic\n4.2.1.1 Chemical bonds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThere are three types of strong chemical bonds: ionic, covalent and\nmetallic. For ionic bonding the particles are oppositely charged ions.\nFor covalent bonding the particles are atoms which share pairs of\nelectrons. For metallic bonding the particles are atoms which share\ndelocalised electrons.\nIonic bonding occurs in compounds formed from metals combined\nwith non-metals.\nCovalent bonding occurs in most non-metallic elements and in\ncompounds of non-metals.\nMetallic bonding occurs in metallic elements and alloys.\nStudents should be able to explain chemical bonding in terms of\nelectrostatic forces and the transfer or sharing of electrons.\n26 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.1.2 Ionic bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhen a metal atom reacts with a non-metal atom electrons in the\nouter shell of the metal atom are transferred. Metal atoms lose\nelectrons to become positively charged ions. Non-metal atoms gain\nelectrons to become negatively charged ions. The ions produced by\nmetals in Groups 1 and 2 and by non-metals in Groups 6 and 7\nhave the electronic structure of a noble gas (Group 0).\nThe electron transfer during the formation of an ionic compound can\nbe represented by a dot and cross diagram, eg for sodium chloride\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nStudents should be able to draw dot and cross diagrams for ionic\ncompounds formed by metals in Groups 1 and 2 with non-metals in\nGroups 6 and 7.\nThe charge on the ions produced by metals in Groups 1 and 2 and\nby non-metals in Groups 6 and 7 relates to the group number of the\nelement in the periodic table.\nStudents should be able to work out the charge on the ions of\nmetals and non-metals from the group number of the element,\nlimited to the metals in Groups 1 and 2, and non-metals in Groups 6\nand 7.\nWS 1.2\n4.2.1.3 Ionic compounds\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAn ionic compound is a giant structure of ions. Ionic compounds are\nheld together by strong electrostatic forces of attraction between\noppositely charged ions. These forces act in all directions in the\nlattice and this is called ionic bonding.\nThe structure of sodium chloride can be represented in the following\nforms:\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 27\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to:\n• deduce that a compound is ionic from a diagram of its\nstructure in one of the specified forms\n• describe the limitations of using dot and cross, ball and stick,\ntwo and three-dimensional diagrams to represent a giant ionic\nstructure\n• work out the empirical formula of an ionic compound from a\ngiven model or diagram that shows the ions in the structure.\nStudents should be familiar with the structure of sodium chloride but\ndo not need to know the structures of other ionic compounds.\nWS 1.2\nMS 4a\nMS 1a, 1c\n28 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.2.1.4 Covalent bonding\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhen atoms share pairs of electrons, they form covalent bonds.\nThese bonds between atoms are strong.\nCovalently bonded substances may consist of small molecules.\nStudents should be able to recognise common substances that\nconsist of small molecules from their chemical formula.\nSome covalently bonded substances have very large molecules,\nsuch as polymers.\nSome covalently bonded substances have giant covalent\nstructures, such as diamond and silicon dioxide.\nThe covalent bonds in molecules and giant structures can be\nrepresented in the following forms:\nPolymers can be represented in the form:\nwhere n is a large number.\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances as\nsmall molecules, polymers\nor giant structures from\ndiagrams showing their\nbonding.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462.           "id": "aqa-gcse-chemistry-8462-v1.1:4-3",
          "code": "4.3",
          "title": "Quantitative chemistry",
          "kind": "chapter",
          "tier": [

          "locator": "Official PDF pages 36-43; 4.3 Quantitative chemistry",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 36-43\nQuantitative chemistry\nChemists use quantitative analysis to determine the formulae of compounds and the equations for\nreactions. Given this information, analysts can then use quantitative methods to determine the\npurity of chemical samples and to monitor the yield from chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be classified in various ways. Identifying different types of chemical\nreaction allows chemists to make sense of how different chemicals react together, to establish\npatterns and to make predictions about the behaviour of other chemicals. Chemical equations\nprovide a means of representing chemical reactions and are a key way for chemists to\ncommunicate chemical ideas.\n36 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.1 Chemical measurements, conservation of mass and the\nquantitative interpretation of chemical equations\n4.3.1.1 Conservation of mass and balanced chemical equations\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe law of conservation of mass states that no atoms are lost or\nmade during a chemical reaction so the mass of the products\nequals the mass of the reactants.\nThis means that chemical reactions can be represented by symbol\nequations which are balanced in terms of the numbers of atoms of\neach element involved on both sides of the equation.\nStudents should understand the use of the multipliers in equations\nin normal script before a formula and in subscript within a formula.\nWS 1.2\n4.3.1.2 Relative formula mass\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative formula mass (Mr) of a compound is the sum of the\nrelative atomic masses of the atoms in the numbers shown in the\nformula.\nIn a balanced chemical equation, the sum of the relative formula\nmasses of the reactants in the quantities shown equals the sum of\nthe relative formula masses of the products in the quantities shown.\nStudents should be able to calculate the percentage by mass in a\ncompound given the relative formula mass and the relative atomic\nmasses.\n4.3.1.3 Mass changes when a reactant or product is a gas\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSome reactions may appear to involve a change in mass but this\ncan usually be explained because a reactant or product is a gas\nand its mass has not been taken into account. For example: when a\nmetal reacts with oxygen the mass of the oxide produced is greater\nthan the mass of the metal or in thermal decompositions of metal\ncarbonates carbon dioxide is produced and escapes into the\natmosphere leaving the metal oxide as the only solid product.\nStudents should be able to explain any observed changes in mass\nin non-enclosed systems during a chemical reaction given the\nbalanced symbol equation for the reaction and explain these\nchanges in terms of the particle model.\nAT 1, 2,6\nOpportunities within\ninvestigation of mass\nchanges using various\napparatus.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 37\n4.3.1.4 Chemical measurements\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhenever a measurement is made there is always some\nuncertainty about the result obtained.\nStudents should be able to:\n• represent the distribution of results and make estimations of\nuncertainty\n• use the range of a set of measurements about the mean as a\nmeasure of uncertainty.\nWS 3.4\n4.3.2 Use of amount of substance in relation to masses of pure\nsubstances\n4.3.2.1 Moles (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nChemical amounts are measured in moles. The symbol for the unit\nmole is mol.\nThe mass of one mole of a substance in grams is numerically equal\nto its relative formula mass.\nOne mole of a substance contains the same number of the stated\nparticles, atoms, molecules or ions as one mole of any other\nsubstance.\nThe number of atoms, molecules or ions in a mole of a given\nsubstance is the Avogadro constant. The value of the Avogadro\nconstant is 6.02 x 1023 per mole.\nStudents should understand that the measurement of amounts in\nmoles can apply to atoms, molecules, ions, electrons, formulae and\nequations, for example that in one mole of carbon (C) the number of\natoms is the same as the number of molecules in one mole of\ncarbon dioxide (CO2).\nWS 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.5, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1b\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in standard\nform.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 3a\nUnderstand and use the\nsymbols: =, <, <<, >>, >, ∝,\n~\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nStudents should be able to use the relative formula mass of a\nsubstance to calculate the number of moles in a given mass of that\nsubstance and vice versa.\nMS 1c\n38 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.2.2 Amounts of substances in equations (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe masses of reactants and products can be calculated from\nbalanced symbol equations.\nChemical equations can be interpreted in terms of moles. For\nexample:\nMg + 2HCI MgCI2 + H2\nshows that one mole of magnesium reacts with two moles of\nhydrochloric acid to produce one mole of magnesium chloride and\none mole of hydrogen gas.\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the masses of substances shown in a balanced\nsymbol equation\n• calculate the masses of reactants and products from the\nbalanced symbol equation and the mass of a given reactant\nor product.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\n4.3.2.3 Using moles to balance equations (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe balancing numbers in a symbol equation can be calculated\nfrom the masses of reactants and products by converting the\nmasses in grams to amounts in moles and converting the numbers\nof moles to simple whole number ratios.\nStudents should be able to balance an equation given the masses\nof reactants and products.\nStudents should be able to change the subject of a mathematical\nequation.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 39\n4.3.2.4 Limiting reactants (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn a chemical reaction involving two reactants, it is common to use\nan excess of one of the reactants to ensure that all of the other\nreactant is used. The reactant that is completely used up is called\nthe limiting reactant because it limits the amount of products.\nStudents should be ab"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 36-43\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to compare ‘nano’ dimensions to typical\ndimensions of atoms and molecules.\nMS 1b\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in standard\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\n4.2.4.2 Uses of nanoparticles\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNanoparticles have many applications in medicine, in electronics, in\ncosmetics and sun creams, as deodorants, and as catalysts. New\napplications for nanoparticulate materials are an important area of\nresearch.\nStudents should consider advantages and disadvantages of the\napplications of these nanoparticulate materials, but do not need to\nknow specific examples or properties other than those specified.\nStudents should be able to:\n• given appropriate information, evaluate the use of\nnanoparticles for a specified purpose\n• explain that there are possible risks associated with the use of\nnanoparticles.\nWS 1.3, 1.4, 1.5\n4.3 Quantitative chemistry\nChemists use quantitative analysis to determine the formulae of compounds and the equations for\nreactions. Given this information, analysts can then use quantitative methods to determine the\npurity of chemical samples and to monitor the yield from chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be classified in various ways. Identifying different types of chemical\nreaction allows chemists to make sense of how different chemicals react together, to establish\npatterns and to make predictions about the behaviour of other chemicals. Chemical equations\nprovide a means of representing chemical reactions and are a key way for chemists to\ncommunicate chemical ideas.\n36 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.1 Chemical measurements, conservation of mass and the\nquantitative interpretation of chemical equations\n4.3.1.1 Conservation of mass and balanced chemical equations\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe law of conservation of mass states that no atoms are lost or\nmade during a chemical reaction so the mass of the products\nequals the mass of the reactants.\nThis means that chemical reactions can be represented by symbol\nequations which are balanced in terms of the numbers of atoms of\neach element involved on both sides of the equation.\nStudents should understand the use of the multipliers in equations\nin normal script before a formula and in subscript within a formula.\nWS 1.2\n4.3.1.2 Relative formula mass\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative formula mass (Mr) of a compound is the sum of the\nrelative atomic masses of the atoms in the numbers shown in the\nformula.\nIn a balanced chemical equation, the sum of the relative formula\nmasses of the reactants in the quantities shown equals the sum of\nthe relative formula masses of the products in the quantities shown.\nStudents should be able to calculate the percentage by mass in a\ncompound given the relative formula mass and the relative atomic\nmasses.\n4.3.1.3 Mass changes when a reactant or product is a gas\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSome reactions may appear to involve a change in mass but this\ncan usually be explained because a reactant or product is a gas\nand its mass has not been taken into account. For example: when a\nmetal reacts with oxygen the mass of the oxide produced is greater\nthan the mass of the metal or in thermal decompositions of metal\ncarbonates carbon dioxide is produced and escapes into the\natmosphere leaving the metal oxide as the only solid product.\nStudents should be able to explain any observed changes in mass\nin non-enclosed systems during a chemical reaction given the\nbalanced symbol equation for the reaction and explain these\nchanges in terms of the particle model.\nAT 1, 2,6\nOpportunities within\ninvestigation of mass\nchanges using various\napparatus.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. The reactant that is completely used up is called\nthe limiting reactant because it limits the amount of products.\nStudents should be able to explain the effect of a limiting quantity of\na reactant on the amount of products it is possible to obtain in terms\nof amounts in moles or masses in grams.\nWS 4.1\n4.3.2.5 Concentration of solutions\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMany chemical reactions take place in solutions. The concentration\nof a solution can be measured in mass per given volume of\nsolution, eg grams per dm3 (g/dm3).\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the mass of solute in a given volume of solution of\nknown concentration in terms of mass per given volume of\nsolution\n• (HT only) explain how the mass of a solute and the volume of\na solution is related to the concentration of the solution.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\n40 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.3 Yield and atom"

When\ncompared with the maximum theoretical amoun"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 36-43\nMass changes when a reactant or product is a gas\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSome reactions may appear to involve a change in mass but this\ncan usually be explained because a reactant or product is a gas\nand its mass has not been taken into account. For example: when a\nmetal reacts with oxygen the mass of the oxide produced is greater\nthan the mass of the metal or in thermal decompositions of metal\ncarbonates carbon dioxide is produced and escapes into the\natmosphere leaving the metal oxide as the only solid product.\nStudents should be able to explain any observed changes in mass\nin non-enclosed systems during a chemical reaction given the\nbalanced symbol equation for the reaction and explain these\nchanges in terms of the particle model.\nAT 1, 2,6\nOpportunities within\ninvestigation of mass\nchanges using various\napparatus.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 37\n4.3.1.4 Chemical measurements\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhenever a measurement is made there is always some\nuncertainty about the result obtained.\nStudents should be able to:\n• represent the distribution of results and make estimations of\nuncertainty\n• use the range of a set of measurements about the mean as a\nmeasure of uncertainty.\nWS 3.4\n4.3.2 Use of amount of substance in relation to masses of pure\nsubstances\n4.3.2.1 Moles (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nChemical amounts are measured in moles. The symbol for the unit\nmole is mol.\nThe mass of one mole of a substance in grams is numerically equal\nto its relative formula mass.\nOne mole of a substance contains the same number of the stated\nparticles, atoms, molecules or ions as one mole of any other\nsubstance.\nThe number of atoms, molecules or ions in a mole of a given\nsubstance is the Avogadro constant. The value of the Avogadro\nconstant is 6.02 x 1023 per mole.\nStudents should understand that the measurement of amounts in\nmoles can apply to atoms, molecules, ions, electrons, formulae and\nequations, for example that in one mole of carbon (C) the number of\natoms is the same as the number of molecules in one mole of\ncarbon dioxide (CO2).\nWS 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.5, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1b\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in standard\nform.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 3a\nUnderstand and use the\nsymbols: =, <, <<, >>, >, ∝,\n~\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nStudents should be able to use the relative formula mass of a\nsubstance to calculate the number of moles in a given mass of that\nsubstance and vice versa.\nMS 1c\n38 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.2.2 Amounts of substances in equations (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe masses of reactants and products can be calculated from\nbalanced symbol equations.\nChemical equations can be interpreted in terms of moles. For\nexample:\nMg + 2HCI MgCI2 + H2\nshows that one mole of magnesium reacts with two moles of\nhydrochloric acid to produce one mole of magnesium chloride and\none mole of hydrogen gas.\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the masses of substances shown in a balanced\nsymbol equation\n• calculate the masses of reactants and products from the\nbalanced symbol equation and the mass of a given reactant\nor product.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\n4.3.2.3 Using moles to balance equations (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe balancing numbers in a symbol equation can be calculated\nfrom the masses of reactants and products by converting the\nmasses in grams to amounts in moles and converting the numbers\nof moles to simple whole number ratios.\nStudents should be able to balance an equation given the masses\nof reactants and products.\nStudents should be able to change the subject of a mathematical\nequation.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 39\n4.3.2.4 Limiting reactants (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn a chemical reaction involving two reactants, it is common to use\nan excess of one of the reactants to ensure that all of the other\nreactant is used. The reactant that is completely used up is called\nthe limiting reactant because it limits the amount of products.\nStudents should be able to explain the effect of a limiting quantity of\na reactant on the amount of products it is possible to obtain in terms\nof amounts in moles or masses in grams.\nWS 4.1\n4.3.2.5 Concentration of solutions\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMany chemical reactions take place in solutions. The concentration\nof a solution can be measured in mass per given volume of\nsolution, eg grams per dm3 (g/dm3).\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the mass of solute in a given volume of solution of\nknown concentration in terms of mass per given volume of\nsolution\n• (HT only) explain how the mass of a solute and the volume of\na solution is related to the concentration of the solution.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\n40 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.3 Yield and atom economy of chemical reactions (chemistry only)\n4.3.3.1 Percentage yield\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nEven though no atoms are gained or lost in a chemical reaction, it is\nnot always possible to obtain the calculated amount of a product\nbecause:\n• the reaction may not go to completion because it is reversible\n• some of the product may be lost when it is separated from the\nreaction mixture\n• some of the reactants may react in ways different to the\nexpected reaction.\nThe amount of a product obtained is known as the yield. When\ncompared with the maximum theoretical amount as a percentage, it\nis called the percentage yield.\n% Yield = Mass o f product actually made\nMaximum theoretical mass o f product\n× 100\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the percentage yield of a product from the actual\nyield of a reaction\n• (HT only) calculate the theoretical mass of a product from a\ngiven mass of reactant and the balanced equation for the\nreaction.\nWS 4.2, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 3b\nCha"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 36-43\nChemical measurements, conservation of mass and the\nquantitative interpretation of chemical equations\n4.3.1.1 Conservation of mass and balanced chemical equations\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe law of conservation of mass states that no atoms are lost or\nmade during a chemical reaction so the mass of the products\nequals the mass of the reactants.\nThis means that chemical reactions can be represented by symbol\nequations which are balanced in terms of the numbers of atoms of\neach element involved on both sides of the equation.\nStudents should understand the use of the multipliers in equations\nin normal script before a formula and in subscript within a formula.\nWS 1.2\n4.3.1.2 Relative formula mass\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative formula mass (Mr) of a compound is the sum of the\nrelative atomic masses of the atoms in the numbers shown in the\nformula.\nIn a balanced chemical equation, the sum of the relative formula\nmasses of the reactants in the quantities shown equals the sum of\nthe relative formula masses of the products in the quantities shown.\nStudents should be able to calculate the percentage by mass in a\ncompound given the relative formula mass and the relative atomic\nmasses.\n4.3.1.3 Mass changes when a reactant or product is a gas\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSome reactions may appear to involve a change in mass but this\ncan usually be explained because a reactant or product is a gas\nand its mass has not been taken into account. For example: when a\nmetal reacts with oxygen the mass of the oxide produced is greater\nthan the mass of the metal or in thermal decompositions of metal\ncarbonates carbon dioxide is produced and escapes into the\natmosphere leaving the metal oxide as the only solid product.\nStudents should be able to explain any observed changes in mass\nin non-enclosed systems during a chemical reaction given the\nbalanced symbol equation for the reaction and explain these\nchanges in terms of the particle model.\nAT 1, 2,6\nOpportunities within\ninvestigation of mass\nchanges using various\napparatus.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. New\napplications for nanoparticulate materials are an important area of\nresearch.\nStudents should consider advantages and disadvantages of the\napplications of these nanoparticulate materials, but do not need to\nknow specific examples or properties other than those specified.\nStudents should be able to:\n• given appropriate information, evaluate the use of\nnanoparticles for a specified purpose\n• explain that there are possible risks associated with the use of\nnanoparticles.\nWS 1.3, 1.4, 1.5\n4.3 Quantitative chemistry\nChemists use quantitative analysis to determine the formulae of compounds and the equations for\nreactions. Given this information, analysts can then use quantitative methods to determine the\npurity of chemical samples and to monitor the yield from chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be classified in various ways. Identifying different types of chemical\nreaction allows chemists to make sense of how different chemicals react together, to establish\npatterns and to make predictions about the behaviour of other chemicals. It is important for\nsustainable development and for economic reasons to use\nreactions with high atom economy.\nThe percentage atom economy of a reaction is calculated using the\nbalanced equation for the reaction as follows:\nRelative f ormula mass o f desired product f rom equation\nSum o f relative f ormula masses o f all reactants f rom equation\n× 100\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the atom economy of a reaction to form a desired\nproduct from the balanced equation\n• (HT only) explain why a particular reaction pathway is chosen\nto produce a specified product given appropriate data such as\natom economy (if not calculated), yield, rate, equilibrium\nposition and usefulness of by-products.\nWS 4.2, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462.           "text": "Official PDF pages 36-43\nAmounts of substances in equations (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe masses of reactants and products can be calculated from\nbalanced symbol equations.\nChemical equations can be interpreted in terms of moles. For\nexample:\nMg + 2HCI MgCI2 + H2\nshows that one mole of magnesium reacts with two moles of\nhydrochloric acid to produce one mole of magnesium chloride and\none mole of hydrogen gas.\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the masses of substances shown in a balanced\nsymbol equation\n• calculate the masses of reactants and products from the\nbalanced symbol equation and the mass of a given reactant\nor product.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\n4.3.2.3 Using moles to balance equations (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe balancing numbers in a symbol equation can be calculated\nfrom the masses of reactants and products by converting the\nmasses in grams to amounts in moles and converting the numbers\nof moles to simple whole number ratios.\nStudents should be able to balance an equation given the masses\nof reactants and products.\nStudents should be able to change the subject of a mathematical\nequation.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 39\n4.3.2.4 Limiting reactants (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn a chemical reaction involving two reactants, it is common to use\nan excess of one of the reactants to ensure that all of the other\nreactant is used. The reactant that is completely used up is called\nthe limiting reactant because it limits the amount of products.\nStudents should be able to explain the effect of a limiting quantity of\na reactant on the amount of products it is possible to obtain in terms\nof amounts in moles or masses in grams.\nWS 4.1\n4.3.2.5 Concentration of solutions\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMany chemical reactions take place in solutions. The concentration\nof a solution can be measured in mass per given volume of\nsolution, eg grams per dm3 (g/dm3).\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the mass of solute in a given volume of solution of\nknown concentration in terms of mass per given volume of\nsolution\n• (HT only) explain how the mass of a solute and the volume of\na solution is related to the concentration of the solution.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\n40 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.3 Yield and atom economy of chemical reactions (chemistry only)\n4.3.3.1 Percentage yield\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nEven though no atoms are gained or lost in a chemical reaction, it is\nnot always possible to obtain the calculated amount of a product\nbecause:\n• the reaction may not go to completion because it is reversible\n• some of the product may be lost when it is separated from the\nreaction mixture\n• some of the reactants may react in ways different to the\nexpected reaction.\nThe amount of a product obtained is known as the yield. When\ncompared with the maximum theoretical amount as a percentage, it\nis called the percentage yield.\n% Yield = Mass o f product actually made\nMaximum theoretical mass o f product\n× 100\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the percentage yield of a product from the actual\nyield of a reaction\n• (HT only) calculate the theoretical mass of a product from a\ngiven mass of reactant and the balanced equation for the\nreaction.\nWS 4.2, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\n4.3.3.2 Atom economy\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe atom economy (atom utilisation) is a measure of the amount of\nstarting materials that end up as useful products. It is important for\nsustainable development and for economic reasons to use\nreactions with high atom economy.\nThe percentage atom economy of a reaction is calculated using the\nbalanced equation for the reaction as follows:\nRelative f ormula mass o f desired product f rom equation\nSum o f relative f ormula masses o f all reactants f rom equation\n× 100\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the atom economy of a reaction to form a desired\nproduct from the balanced equation\n• (HT only) explain why a particular reaction pathway is chosen\nto produce a specified product given appropriate data such as\natom economy (if not calculated), yield, rate, equilibrium\nposition and usefulness of by-products.\nWS 4.2, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 41\n4.3.4 Using concentrations of solutions in mol/dm3 (chemistry only)\n(HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe concentration of a solution can be measured in mol/dm3.\nThe amount in moles of solute or the mass in grams of solute in a\ngiven volume of solution can be calculated from its concentration in\nmol/dm3.\nIf the volumes of two solutions that react completely are known and\nthe concentration of one solution is known, the concentration of the\nother solution can be calculated.\nStudents should be able to explain how the concentration of a\nsolution in mol/dm3 is related to the mass of the solute and the\nvolume of the solution.\nWS 4.2, 4.3, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\nAT 1, 3, 8\nOpportunities within\ntitrations including to\ndetermine concentrations of\nstrong acids and alkalis.\n42 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.5 Use of amount of substance in relation to volumes of gases\n(chemistry only) (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nEqual amounts in moles of gases occupy the same volume under\nthe same conditions of temperature and pressure.\nThe volume of one mole of any gas at room temperature and\npressure (20oC and 1 atmosphere pressure) is 24 dm3.\nThe volumes of gaseous reactants and products can be calculated\nfrom the balanced equation for the reaction.\nS"

logically. Knowing about these different\nchemical changes meant that scientists could begin to predict exactly what new substances would\nbe formed and use this knowledge to develop a wide range of different materials and processes.           "locator": "Official PDF pages 36-43; 4.3.3 Yield and atom economy of chemical reactions (chemistry only)",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 36-43\nYield and atom economy of chemical reactions (chemistry only)\n4.3.3.1 Percentage yield\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nEven though no atoms are gained or lost in a chemical reaction, it is\nnot always possible to obtain the calculated amount of a product\nbecause:\n• the reaction may not go to completion because it is reversible\n• some of the product may be lost when it is separated from the\nreaction mixture\n• some of the reactants may react in ways different to the\nexpected reaction.\nThe amount of a product obtained is known as the yield. When\ncompared with the maximum theoretical amount as a percentage, it\nis called the percentage yield.\n% Yield = Mass o f product actually made\nMaximum theoretical mass o f product\n× 100\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the percentage yield of a product from the actual\nyield of a reaction\n• (HT only) calculate the theoretical mass of a product from a\ngiven mass of reactant and the balanced equation for the\nreaction.\nWS 4.2, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\n4.3.3.2 Atom economy\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe atom economy (atom utilisation) is a measure of the amount of\nstarting materials that end up as useful products. It is important for\nsustainable development and for economic reasons to use\nreactions with high atom economy.\nThe percentage atom economy of a reaction is calculated using the\nbalanced equation for the reaction as follows:\nRelative f ormula mass o f desired product f rom equation\nSum o f relative f ormula masses o f all reactants f rom equation\n× 100\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the atom economy of a reaction to form a desired\nproduct from the balanced equation\n• (HT only) explain why a particular reaction pathway is chosen\nto produce a specified product given appropriate data such as\natom economy (if not calculated), yield, rate, equilibrium\nposition and usefulness of by-products.\nWS 4.2, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 41\n4.3.4 Using concentrations of solutions in mol/dm3 (chemistry only)\n(HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe concentration of a solution can be measured in mol/dm3.\nThe amount in moles of solute or the mass in grams of solute in a\ngiven volume of solution can be calculated from its concentration in\nmol/dm3.\nIf the volumes of two solutions that react completely are known and\nthe concentration of one solution is known, the concentration of the\nother solution can be calculated.\nStudents should be able to explain how the concentration of a\nsolution in mol/dm3 is related to the mass of the solute and the\nvolume of the solution.\nWS 4.2, 4.3, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\nAT 1, 3, 8\nOpportunities within\ntitrations including to\ndetermine concentrations of\nstrong acids and alkalis.\n42 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.5 Use of amount of substance in relation to volumes of gases\n(chemistry only) (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nEqual amounts in moles of gases occupy the same volume under\nthe same conditions of temperature and pressure.\nThe volume of one mole of any gas at room temperature and\npressure (20oC and 1 atmosphere pressure) is 24 dm3.\nThe volumes of gaseous reactants and products can be calculated\nfrom the balanced equation for the reaction.\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the volume of a gas at room temperature and\npressure from its mass and relative formula mass\n• calculate volumes of gaseous reactants and products from a\nbalanced equation and a given volume of a gaseous reactant\nor product\n• change the subject of a mathematical equation.\nWS 1.2, 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\n4.4 Chemical changes\nUnderstanding of chemical changes began when people began experimenting with chemical\nreactions in a systematic way and organizing their results logically. Knowing about these different\nchemical changes meant that scientists could begin to predict exactly what new substances would\nbe formed and use this knowledge to develop a wide range of different materials and processes. It\nalso helped biochemists to understand the complex reactions that take place in living organisms.\nThe extraction of important resources from the earth makes use of the way that some elements\nand compounds react with each other and how easily they can be ‘pulled apart’.\n4.4.1 Reactivity of metals\n4.4.1.1 Metal oxides\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMetals react with oxygen to produce metal oxides. The reactions\nare oxidation reactions because the metals gain oxygen.\nStudents should be able to explain reduction and oxidation in terms\nof loss or gain of oxygen.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 43"

          "code": "4.3.4",
          "title": "Using concentrations of solutions in mol/dm3 (chemistry only) (HT only)",

          "tier": [
            "Higher"

          "locator": "Official PDF pages 36-43; 4.3.4 Using concentrations of solutions in mol/dm3 (chemistry only) (HT only)",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 36-43\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nStudents should be able to compare ‘nano’ dimensions to typical\ndimensions of atoms and molecules.\nMS 1b\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in standard\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\n4.2.4.2 Uses of nanoparticles\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nNanoparticles have many applications in medicine, in electronics, in\ncosmetics and sun creams, as deodorants, and as catalysts. New\napplications for nanoparticulate materials are an important area of\nresearch.\nStudents should consider advantages and disadvantages of the\napplications of these nanoparticulate materials, but do not need to\nknow specific examples or properties other than those specified.\nStudents should be able to:\n• given appropriate information, evaluate the use of\nnanoparticles for a specified purpose\n• explain that there are possible risks associated with the use of\nnanoparticles.\nWS 1.3, 1.4, 1.5\n4.3 Quantitative chemistry\nChemists use quantitative analysis to determine the formulae of compounds and the equations for\nreactions. Given this information, analysts can then use quantitative methods to determine the\npurity of chemical samples and to monitor the yield from chemical reactions.\nChemical reactions can be classified in various ways. Identifying different types of chemical\nreaction allows chemists to make sense of how different chemicals react together, to establish\npatterns and to make predictions about the behaviour of other chemicals. Chemical equations\nprovide a means of representing chemical reactions and are a key way for chemists to\ncommunicate chemical ideas.\n36 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.1 Chemical measurements, conservation of mass and the\nquantitative interpretation of chemical equations\n4.3.1.1 Conservation of mass and balanced chemical equations\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe law of conservation of mass states that no atoms are lost or\nmade during a chemical reaction so the mass of the products\nequals the mass of the reactants.\nThis means that chemical reactions can be represented by symbol\nequations which are balanced in terms of the numbers of atoms of\neach element involved on both sides of the equation.\nStudents should understand the use of the multipliers in equations\nin normal script before a formula and in subscript within a formula.\nWS 1.2\n4.3.1.2 Relative formula mass\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe relative formula mass (Mr) of a compound is the sum of the\nrelative atomic masses of the atoms in the numbers shown in the\nformula.\nIn a balanced chemical equation, the sum of the relative formula\nmasses of the reactants in the quantities shown equals the sum of\nthe relative formula masses of the products in the quantities shown.\nStudents should be able to calculate the percentage by mass in a\ncompound given the relative formula mass and the relative atomic\nmasses.\n4.3.1.3 Mass changes when a reactant or product is a gas\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSome reactions may appear to involve a change in mass but this\ncan usually be explained because a reactant or product is a gas\nand its mass has not been taken into account. For example: when a\nmetal reacts with oxygen the mass of the oxide produced is greater\nthan the mass of the metal or in thermal decompositions of metal\ncarbonates carbon dioxide is produced and escapes into the\natmosphere leaving the metal oxide as the only solid product.\nStudents should be able to explain any observed changes in mass\nin non-enclosed systems during a chemical reaction given the\nbalanced symbol equation for the reaction and explain these\nchanges in terms of the particle model.\nAT 1, 2,6\nOpportunities within\ninvestigation of mass\nchanges using various\napparatus.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 37\n4.3.1.4 Chemical measurements\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWhenever a measurement is made there is always some\nuncertainty about the result obtained.\nStudents should be able to:\n• represent the distribution of results and make estimations of\nuncertainty\n• use the range of a set of measurements about the mean as a\nmeasure of uncertainty.\nWS 3.4\n4.3.2 Use of amount of substance in relation to masses of pure\nsubstances\n4.3.2.1 Moles (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nChemical amounts are measured in moles. The symbol for the unit\nmole is mol.\nThe mass of one mole of a substance in grams is numerically equal\nto its relative formula mass.\nOne mole of a substance contains the same number of the stated\nparticles, atoms, molecules or ions as one mole of any other\nsubstance.\nThe number of atoms, molecules or ions in a mole of a given\nsubstance is the Avogadro constant. The value of the Avogadro\nconstant is 6.02 x 1023 per mole.\nStudents should understand that the measurement of amounts in\nmoles can apply to atoms, molecules, ions, electrons, formulae and\nequations, for example that in one mole of carbon (C) the number of\natoms is the same as the number of molecules in one mole of\ncarbon dioxide (CO2).\nWS 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.5, 4.6\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1b\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in standard\nform.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 3a\nUnderstand and use the\nsymbols: =, <, <<, >>, >, ∝,\n~\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nStudents should be able to use the relative formula mass of a\nsubstance to calculate the number of moles in a given mass of that\nsubstance and vice versa.\nMS 1c\n38 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.3.2.2 Amounts of substances in equations (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe masses of reactants and products can be calculated from\nbalanced symbol equations.\nChemical equations can be interpreted in terms of moles. For\nexample:\nMg + 2HCI MgCI2 + H2\nshows that one mole of magnesium reacts with two moles of\nhydrochloric acid to produce one mole of magnesium chloride and\none mole of hydrogen gas.\nStudents should be able to:\n• calculate the masses of substances shown in a balanced\nsymbol equation\n• calculate the masses of reactants and products from the\nbalanced symbol equation and the mass of a given reactant\nor product.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 3b\nChange the subject of an\nequation.\nMS 3c\nSubstitute numerical values\ninto algebraic equations\nusing appropriate units for\nphysical quantities.\n4.3.2.3 Using moles to balance equations (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe balancing numbers in a symbol equation can be calculated\nfrom the masses of reac"
