          "locator": "Official PDF pages 61-70; 4.7.1.1 Crude oil, hydrocarbons and alkanes",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 61-70\nCrude oil, hydrocarbons and alkanes\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCrude oil is a finite resource found in rocks. Crude oil is the remains\nof an ancient biomass consisting mainly of plankton that was buried\nin mud.\nCrude oil is a mixture of a very large number of compounds. Most of\nthe compounds in crude oil are hydrocarbons, which are molecules\nmade up of hydrogen and carbon atoms only.\nMost of the hydrocarbons in crude oil are hydrocarbons called\nalkanes. The general formula for the homologous series of alkanes\nis CnH2n+2\nThe first four members of the alkanes are methane, ethane,\npropane and butane.\nAlkane molecules can be represented in the following forms:\nC2H6 or\nStudents should be able to recognise substances as alkanes given\ntheir formulae in these forms.\nStudents do not need to know the names of specific alkanes other\nthan methane, ethane, propane and butane.\nWS 1.2\nMake models of alkane\nmolecules using the\nmolecular modelling kits.\n62 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.7.1.2 Fractional distillation and petrochemicals\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe many hydrocarbons in crude oil may be separated into\nfractions, each of which contains molecules with a similar number of\ncarbon atoms, by fractional distillation.\nThe fractions can be processed to produce fuels and feedstock for\nthe petrochemical industry.\nMany of the fuels on which we depend for our modern lifestyle,\nsuch as petrol, diesel oil, kerosene, heavy fuel oil and liquefied\npetroleum gases, are produced from crude oil.\nMany useful materials on which modern life depends are produced\nby the petrochemical industry, such as solvents, lubricants,\npolymers, detergents.\nThe vast array of natural and synthetic carbon compounds occur\ndue to the ability of carbon atoms to form families of similar\ncompounds.\nStudents should be able to explain how fractional distillation works\nin terms of evaporation and condensation.\nKnowledge of the names of other specific fractions or fuels is not\nrequired.\nWS 1.2\n4.7.1.3 Properties of hydrocarbons\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSome properties of hydrocarbons depend on the size of their\nmolecules, including boiling point, viscosity and flammability. These\nproperties influence how hydrocarbons are used as fuels.\nStudents should be able to recall how boiling point, viscosity and\nflammability change with increasing molecular size.\nThe combustion of hydrocarbon fuels releases energy. During\ncombustion, the carbon and hydrogen in the fuels are oxidised. The\ncomplete combustion of a hydrocarbon produces carbon dioxide\nand water.\nStudents should be able to write balanced equations for the\ncomplete combustion of hydrocarbons with a given formula.\nKnowledge of trends in properties of hydrocarbons is limited to:\n• boiling points\n• viscosity\n• flammability.\nWS 1.2, 4.1\nInvestigate the properties of\ndifferent hydrocarbons.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 63\n4.7.1.4 Cracking and alkenes\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nHydrocarbons can be broken down (cracked) to produce smaller,\nmore useful molecules.\nCracking can be done by various methods including catalytic\ncracking and steam cracking.\nStudents should be able to describe in general terms the conditions\nused for catalytic cracking and steam cracking.\nThe products of cracking include alkanes and another type of\nhydrocarbon called alkenes.\nAlkenes are more reactive than alkanes and react with bromine\nwater, which is used as a test for alkenes.\nStudents should be able to recall the colour change when bromine\nwater reacts with an alkene.\nThere is a high demand for fuels with small molecules and so some\nof the products of cracking are useful as fuels.\nAlkenes are used to produce polymers and as starting materials for\nthe production of many other chemicals.\nStudents should be able to balance chemical equations as\nexamples of cracking given the formulae of the reactants and\nproducts.\nStudents should be able to give examples to illustrate the\nusefulness of cracking. They should also be able to explain how\nmodern life depends on the uses of hydrocarbons.\n(For Combined Science: Trilogy and Synergy students do not need\nto know the formulae or names of individual alkenes.)\nWS 1.2\n64 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.7.2 Reactions of alkenes and alcohols (chemistry only)\n4.7.2.1 Structure and formulae of alkenes\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlkenes are hydrocarbons with a double carbon-carbon bond. The\ngeneral formula for the homologous series of alkenes is CnH2n\nAlkene molecules are unsaturated because they contain two fewer\nhydrogen atoms than the alkane with the same number of carbon\natoms.\nThe first four members of the homologous series of alkenes are\nethene, propene, butene and pentene.\nAlkene molecules can be represented in the following forms:\nC3H6\nor\nStudents do not need to know the names of individual alkenes other\nthan ethene, propene, butene and pentene.\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances that\nare alkenes from their\nnames or from given\nformulae in these forms.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n4.7.2.2 Reactions of alkenes\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlkenes are hydrocarbons with the functional group C=C.\nIt is the generality of reactions of functional groups that determine\nthe reactions of organic compounds.\nAlkenes react with oxygen in combustion reactions in the same way\nas other hydrocarbons, but they tend to burn in air with smoky\nflames because of incomplete combustion.\nAlkenes react with hydrogen, water and the halogens, by the\naddition of atoms across the carbon-carbon double bond so that the\ndouble bond becomes a single carbon-carbon bond.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe the reactions and conditions for the addition of\nhydrogen, water and halogens to alkenes\n• draw fully displayed structural formulae of the first four\nmembers of the alkenes and the products of their addition\nreactions with hydrogen, water, chlorine, bromine and iodine.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 65\n4.7.2.3 Alcohols\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlcohols contain the functional group –OH.\nMethanol, ethanol, propanol and butanol are the first four members\nof a homologous series of alcohols.\nAlcohols can be represented in the following forms:\nCH3CH2OH\nor\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe what happens when any of the first four alcohols\nreact with sodium, burn in air, are added to water, react with\nan oxidising agent\n• recall the"

          "locator": "Official PDF pages 61-70; 4.7.2 Reactions of alkenes and alcohols (chemistry only)",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 61-70\nReactions of alkenes and alcohols (chemistry only)\n4.7.2.1 Structure and formulae of alkenes\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlkenes are hydrocarbons with a double carbon-carbon bond. The\ngeneral formula for the homologous series of alkenes is CnH2n\nAlkene molecules are unsaturated because they contain two fewer\nhydrogen atoms than the alkane with the same number of carbon\natoms.\nThe first four members of the homologous series of alkenes are\nethene, propene, butene and pentene.\nAlkene molecules can be represented in the following forms:\nC3H6\nor\nStudents do not need to know the names of individual alkenes other\nthan ethene, propene, butene and pentene.\nWS 1.2\nRecognise substances that\nare alkenes from their\nnames or from given\nformulae in these forms.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n4.7.2.2 Reactions of alkenes\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlkenes are hydrocarbons with the functional group C=C.\nIt is the generality of reactions of functional groups that determine\nthe reactions of organic compounds.\nAlkenes react with oxygen in combustion reactions in the same way\nas other hydrocarbons, but they tend to burn in air with smoky\nflames because of incomplete combustion.\nAlkenes react with hydrogen, water and the halogens, by the\naddition of atoms across the carbon-carbon double bond so that the\ndouble bond becomes a single carbon-carbon bond.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe the reactions and conditions for the addition of\nhydrogen, water and halogens to alkenes\n• draw fully displayed structural formulae of the first four\nmembers of the alkenes and the products of their addition\nreactions with hydrogen, water, chlorine, bromine and iodine.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 65\n4.7.2.3 Alcohols\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlcohols contain the functional group –OH.\nMethanol, ethanol, propanol and butanol are the first four members\nof a homologous series of alcohols.\nAlcohols can be represented in the following forms:\nCH3CH2OH\nor\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe what happens when any of the first four alcohols\nreact with sodium, burn in air, are added to water, react with\nan oxidising agent\n• recall the main uses of these alcohols.\nAqueous solutions of ethanol are produced when sugar solutions\nare fermented using yeast.\nStudents should know the conditions used for fermentation of sugar\nusing yeast.\nStudents should be able to recognise alcohols from their names or\nfrom given formulae.\nStudents do not need to know the names of individual alcohols\nother than methanol, ethanol, propanol and butanol.\nStudents are not expected to write balanced chemical equations for\nthe reactions of alcohols other than for combustion reactions.\nAT 2, 5, 6\nOpportunities when\ninvestigating reactions of\nalcohols.\n66 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.7.2.4 Carboxylic acids\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCarboxylic acids have the functional group –COOH.\nThe first four members of a homologous series of carboxylic acids\nare methanoic acid, ethanoic acid, propanoic acid and butanoic\nacid.\nThe structures of carboxylic acids can be represented in the\nfollowing forms:\nCH3COOH\nor\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe what happens when any of the first four carboxylic\nacids react with carbonates, dissolve in water, react with\nalcohols\n• (HT only) explain why carboxylic acids are weak acids in\nterms of ionisation and pH (see Strong and weak acids (HT\nonly) (page 48)).\nStudents should be able to recognise carboxylic acids from their\nnames or from given formulae.\nStudents do not need to know the names of individual carboxylic\nacids other than methanoic acid, ethanoic acid, propanoic acid and\nbutanoic acid.\nStudents are not expected to write balanced chemical equations for\nthe reactions of carboxylic acids.\nStudents do not need to know the names of esters other than ethyl\nethanoate.\nAT 2, 5, 6\nOpportunities within\ninvestigation of the\nreactions of carboxylic\nacids.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 67\n4.7.3 Synthetic and naturally occurring polymers (chemistry only)\n4.7.3.1 Addition polymerisation\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlkenes can be used to make polymers such as poly(ethene) and\npoly(propene) by addition polymerisation.\nIn addition polymerisation reactions, many small molecules\n(monomers) join together to form very large molecules (polymers).\nFor example:\nIn addition polymers the repeating unit has the same atoms as the\nmonomer because no other molecule is formed in the reaction.\nWS 1.2\nUse models to represent\naddition polymerisation.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recognise addition polymers and monomers from diagrams in\nthe forms shown and from the presence of the functional\ngroup C=C in the monomers\n• draw diagrams to represent the formation of a polymer from a\ngiven alkene monomer\n• relate the repeating unit to the monomer.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n68 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.7.3.2 Condensation polymerisation (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCondensation polymerisation involves monomers with two\nfunctional groups. When these types of monomers react they join\ntogether, usually losing small molecules such as water, and so the\nreactions are called condensation reactions.\nThe simplest polymers are produced from two different monomers\nwith two of the same functional groups on each monomer.\nFor example:\nethanediol\nand\nhexanedioic acid\npolymerise to produce a polyester:\nWS 1.2\nUse models to represent\ncondensation\npolymerisation.\nStudents should be able to explain the basic principles of\ncondensation polymerisation by reference to the functional groups\nin the monomers and the repeating units in the polymers.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 69\n4.7.3.3 Amino acids (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAmino acids have two different functional groups in a molecule.\nAmino acids react by condensation polymerisation to produce\npolypeptides.\nFor example: glycine is H2NCH2COOH and polymerises to produce\nthe polypeptide\nDifferent amino acids can be combined in the same chain to\nproduce proteins.\n4.7.3.4 DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 61-70\nCarboxylic acids\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCarboxylic acids have the functional group –COOH.\nThe first four members of a homologous series of carboxylic acids\nare methanoic acid, ethanoic acid, propanoic acid and butanoic\nacid.\nThe structures of carboxylic acids can be represented in the\nfollowing forms:\nCH3COOH\nor\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe what happens when any of the first four carboxylic\nacids react with carbonates, dissolve in water, react with\nalcohols\n• (HT only) explain why carboxylic acids are weak acids in\nterms of ionisation and pH (see Strong and weak acids (HT\nonly) (page 48)).\nStudents should be able to recognise carboxylic acids from their\nnames or from given formulae.\nStudents do not need to know the names of individual carboxylic\nacids other than methanoic acid, ethanoic acid, propanoic acid and\nbutanoic acid.\nStudents are not expected to write balanced chemical equations for\nthe reactions of carboxylic acids.\nStudents do not need to know the names of esters other than ethyl\nethanoate.\nAT 2, 5, 6\nOpportunities within\ninvestigation of the\nreactions of carboxylic\nacids.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 67\n4.7.3 Synthetic and naturally occurring polymers (chemistry only)\n4.7.3.1 Addition polymerisation\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlkenes can be used to make polymers such as poly(ethene) and\npoly(propene) by addition polymerisation.\nIn addition polymerisation reactions, many small molecules\n(monomers) join together to form very large molecules (polymers).\nFor example:\nIn addition polymers the repeating unit has the same atoms as the\nmonomer because no other molecule is formed in the reaction.\nWS 1.2\nUse models to represent\naddition polymerisation.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recognise addition polymers and monomers from diagrams in\nthe forms shown and from the presence of the functional\ngroup C=C in the monomers\n• draw diagrams to represent the formation of a polymer from a\ngiven alkene monomer\n• relate the repeating unit to the monomer.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\n68 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.7.3.2 Condensation polymerisation (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCondensation polymerisation involves monomers with two\nfunctional groups. When these types of monomers react they join\ntogether, usually losing small molecules such as water, and so the\nreactions are called condensation reactions.\nThe simplest polymers are produced from two different monomers\nwith two of the same functional groups on each monomer.\nFor example:\nethanediol\nand\nhexanedioic acid\npolymerise to produce a polyester:\nWS 1.2\nUse models to represent\ncondensation\npolymerisation.\nStudents should be able to explain the basic principles of\ncondensation polymerisation by reference to the functional groups\nin the monomers and the repeating units in the polymers.\nMS 5b\nVisualise and represent 2D\nand 3D forms including two-\ndimensional representations\nof 3D objects.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 69\n4.7.3.3 Amino acids (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAmino acids have two different functional groups in a molecule.\nAmino acids react by condensation polymerisation to produce\npolypeptides.\nFor example: glycine is H2NCH2COOH and polymerises to produce\nthe polypeptide\nDifferent amino acids can be combined in the same chain to\nproduce proteins.\n4.7.3.4 DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and other naturally occurring polymers\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nDNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a large molecule essential for life.\nDNA encodes genetic instructions for the development and\nfunctioning of living organisms and viruses.\nMost DNA molecules are two polymer chains, made from four\ndifferent monomers called nucleotides, in the form of a double helix.\nOther naturally occurring polymers important for life include\nproteins, starch and cellulose.\nStudents should be able to name the types of monomers from\nwhich these naturally occurring polymers are made.\n4.8 Chemical analysis\nAnalysts have developed a range of qualitative tests to detect specific chemicals. The tests are\nbased on reactions that produce a gas with distinctive properties, or a colour change or an\ninsoluble solid that appears as a precipitate.\nInstrumental methods provide fast, sensitive and accurate means of analysing chemicals, and are\nparticularly useful when the amount of chemical being analysed is small. Forensic scientists and\ndrug control scientists rely on such instrumental methods in their work.\n70 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 70-75\nChemical analysis\nAnalysts have developed a range of qualitative tests to detect specific chemicals. The tests are\nbased on reactions that produce a gas with distinctive properties, or a colour change or an\ninsoluble solid that appears as a precipitate.\nInstrumental methods provide fast, sensitive and accurate means of analysing chemicals, and are\nparticularly useful when the amount of chemical being analysed is small. Forensic scientists and\ndrug control scientists rely on such instrumental methods in their work.\n70 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.8.1 Purity, formulations and chromatography\n4.8.1.1 Pure substances\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn chemistry, a pure substance is a single element or compound,\nnot mixed with any other substance.\nPure elements and compounds melt and boil at specific\ntemperatures. Melting point and boiling point data can be used to\ndistinguish pure substances from mixtures.\nIn everyday language, a pure substance can mean a substance\nthat has had nothing added to it, so it is unadulterated and in its\nnatural state, eg pure milk.\nStudents should be able to use melting point and boiling point data\nto distinguish pure from impure substances.\nWS 2.2, 4.1\n4.8.1.2 Formulations\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nA formulation is a mixture that has been designed as a useful\nproduct. Many products are complex mixtures in which each\nchemical has a particular purpose. Formulations are made by\nmixing the components in carefully measured quantities to ensure\nthat the product has the required properties. Formulations include\nfuels, cleaning agents, paints, medicines, alloys, fertilisers and\nfoods.\nStudents should be able to identify formulations given appropriate\ninformation.\nStudents do not need to know the names of components in\nproprietary products.\nWS 1.4, 2.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 71\n4.8.1.3 Chromatography\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nChromatography can be used to separate mixtures and can give\ninformation to help identify substances. Chromatography involves a\nstationary phase and a mobile phase. Separation depends on the\ndistribution of substances between the phases.\nThe ratio of the distance moved by a compound (centre of spot from\norigin) to the distance moved by the solvent can be expressed as its\nRf value:\nRf = distance moved by substance\ndistance moved by solvent\nDifferent compounds have different Rf values in different solvents,\nwhich can be used to help identify the compounds. The compounds\nin a mixture may separate into different spots depending on the\nsolvent but a pure compound will produce a single spot in all\nsolvents.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how paper chromatography separates mixtures\n• suggest how chromatographic methods can be used for\ndistinguishing pure substances from impure substances\n• interpret chromatograms and determine Rf values from\nchromatograms\nWS 2.2, 3.1, 2, 3\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\n• provide answers to an appropriate number of significant\nfigures.\nMS 2a\nRequired practical 6: investigate how paper chromatography can be used to separate and tell the\ndifference between coloured substances. Students should calculate Rf values.\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 1 and 4.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities for skills development (page 107).\n4.8.2 Identification of common gases\n4.8.2.1 Test for hydrogen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for hydrogen uses a burning splint held at the open end of\na test tube of the gas. Hydrogen burns rapidly with a pop sound.\n72 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.8.2.2 Test for oxygen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for oxygen uses a glowing splint inserted into a test tube of\nthe gas. The splint relights in oxygen.\n4.8.2.3 Test for carbon dioxide\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for carbon dioxide uses an aqueous solution of calcium\nhydroxide (lime water). When carbon dioxide is shaken with or\nbubbled through limewater the limewater turns milky (cloudy).\n4.8.2.4 Test for chlorine\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for chlorine uses litmus paper. When damp litmus paper is\nput into chlorine gas the litmus paper is bleached and turns white.\n4.8.3 Identification of ions by chemical and spectroscopic means\n(chemistry only)\n4.8.3.1 Flame tests\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFlame tests can be used to identify some metal ions (cations).\nLithium, sodium, potassium, calcium and copper compounds\nproduce distinctive colours in flame tests:\n• lithium compounds result in a crimson flame\n• sodium compounds result in a yellow flame\n• potassium compounds result in a lilac flame\n• calcium compounds result in an orange-red flame\n• copper compounds result in a green flame.\nIf a sample containing a mixture of ions is used some flame colours\ncan be masked.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to\ninvestigate flame colours.\nStudents should be able to identify species from the results of the\ntests in 4.8.3.1 to 4.8.3.5.\nFlame colours of other metal ions are not required knowledge.\nWS 2.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 73\n4.8.3.2 Metal hydroxides\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSodium hydroxide solution can be used to identify some metal ions\n(cations).\nSolutions of aluminium, calcium and magnesium ions form white\nprecipitates when sodium hydroxide solution is added but only the\naluminium hydroxide precipitate dissolves in excess sodium\nhydroxide solution.\nSolutions of copper(II), iron(II) and iron(III) ions form coloured\nprecipitates when sodium hydroxide solution is added.\nCopper(II) forms a blue precipitate, iron(II) a green precipitate and\niron(III) a brown precipitate.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to make\nprecipitates of metal\nhydroxides.\nStudents should be able to write balanced equations for the\nreactions to produce the insoluble hydroxides.\nStudents are not expected to write equations for the production of\nsodium aluminate.\nWS 2.2\n4.8.3.3 Carbonates\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCarbonates react with dilute acids to form carbon dioxide gas.\nCarbon dioxide can be identified with limewater.\n4.8.3.4 Halides\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nHalide ions in solution produce precipitates with silver nitrate\nsolution in the presence of dilut"

Students should calculate Rf values.\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 1 and 4.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities for skills development (page 107).\n4.8.2 Identification of common gases\n4.8.2.1 Test for hydrogen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for hydrogen uses a burning splint held at the open end of\na test tube of the gas. Hydrogen burns rapidly with a pop sound.\n72 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.8.2.2 Test for oxygen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for oxygen uses a glowing splint inserted into a test tube of\nthe gas. The splint relights in oxygen.\n4.8.2.3 Test for carbon dioxide\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for carbon dioxide uses an aqueous solution of calcium\nhydroxide (lime water). When carbon dioxide is shaken with or\nbubbled through limewater the limewater turns milky (cloudy).\n4.8.2.4 Test for chlorine\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for chlorine uses litmus paper. When damp litmus paper is\nput into chlorine gas the litmus paper is bleached and turns white.\n4.8.3 Identification of ions by chemical and spectroscopic means\n(chemistry only)\n4.8.3.1 Flame tests\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFlame tests can be used to identify some metal ions (cations).\nLithium, sodium, potassium, calcium and copper compounds\nproduce distinctive colours in flame tests:\n• lithium compounds result in a crimson flame\n• sodium compounds result in a yellow flame\n• potassium compounds result in a lilac flame\n• calcium compounds result in an orange-red flame\n• copper compounds result in a green flame.\nIf a sample containing a mixture of ions is used some flame colours\ncan be masked.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to\ninvestigate flame colours.\nStudents should be able to identify species from the results of the\ntests in 4.8.3.1 to 4.8.3.5.\nFlame colours of other metal ions are not required knowledge.\nWS 2.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 73\n4.8.3.2 Metal hydroxides\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSodium hydroxide solution can be used to identify some metal ions\n(cations).\nSolutions of aluminium, calcium and magnesium ions form white\nprecipitates when sodium hydroxide solution is added but only the\naluminium hydroxide precipitate dissolves in excess sodium\nhydroxide solution.\nSolutions of copper(II), iron(II) and iron(III) ions form coloured\nprecipitates when sodium hydroxide solution is added.\nCopper(II) forms a blue precipitate, iron(II) a green precipitate and\niron(III) a brown precipitate.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to make\nprecipitates of metal\nhydroxides.\nStudents should be able to write balanced equations for the\nreactions to produce the insoluble hydroxides.\nStudents are not expected to write equations for the production of\nsodium aluminate.\nWS 2.2\n4.8.3.3 Carbonates\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCarbonates react with dilute acids to form carbon dioxide gas.\nCarbon dioxide can be identified with limewater.\n4.8.3.4 Halides\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nHalide ions in solution produce precipitates with silver nitrate\nsolution in the presence of dilute nitric acid. Silver chloride is white,\nsilver bromide is cream and silver iodide is yellow.\n4.8.3.5 Sulfates\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSulfate ions in solution produce a white precipitate with barium\nchloride solution in the presence of dilute hydrochloric acid.\nRequired practical 7: use of chemical tests to identify the ions in unknown single ionic\ncompounds covering the ions from sections Flame tests (page 73) to Sulfates (page 74).\n74 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 1 and 8.\nThis pr"

          "id": "aqa-gcse-chemistry-8462-v1.1:4-8-1-1",
          "code": "4.8.1.1",
          "title": "Pure substances",
          "kind": "topic",
          "tier": [
            "Foundation",
            "Higher"
          ],
          "locator": "Official PDF pages 70-75; 4.8.1.1 Pure substances",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 70-75\nPure substances\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn chemistry, a pure substance is a single element or compound,\nnot mixed with any other substance.\nPure elements and compounds melt and boil at specific\ntemperatures. Melting point and boiling point data can be used to\ndistinguish pure substances from mixtures.\nIn everyday language, a pure substance can mean a substance\nthat has had nothing added to it, so it is unadulterated and in its\nnatural state, eg pure milk.\nStudents should be able to use melting point and boiling point data\nto distinguish pure from impure substances.\nWS 2.2, 4.1\n4.8.1.2 Formulations\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nA formulation is a mixture that has been designed as a useful\nproduct. Many products are complex mixtures in which each\nchemical has a particular purpose. Formulations are made by\nmixing the components in carefully measured quantities to ensure\nthat the product has the required properties. Formulations include\nfuels, cleaning agents, paints, medicines, alloys, fertilisers and\nfoods.\nStudents should be able to identify formulations given appropriate\ninformation.\nStudents do not need to know the names of components in\nproprietary products.\nWS 1.4, 2.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 71\n4.8.1.3 Chromatography\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nChromatography can be used to separate mixtures and can give\ninformation to help identify substances. Chromatography involves a\nstationary phase and a mobile phase. Separation depends on the\ndistribution of substances between the phases.\nThe ratio of the distance moved by a compound (centre of spot from\norigin) to the distance moved by the solvent can be expressed as its\nRf value:\nRf = distance moved by substance\ndistance moved by solvent\nDifferent compounds have different Rf values in different solvents,\nwhich can be used to help identify the compounds. The compounds\nin a mixture may separate into different spots depending on the\nsolvent but a pure compound will produce a single spot in all\nsolvents.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how paper chromatography separates mixtures\n• suggest how chromatographic methods can be used for\ndistinguishing pure substances from impure substances\n• interpret chromatograms and determine Rf values from\nchromatograms\nWS 2.2, 3.1, 2, 3\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\n• provide answers to an appropriate number of significant\nfigures.\nMS 2a\nRequired practical 6: investigate how paper chromatography can be used to separate and tell the\ndifference between coloured substances. Students should calculate Rf values.\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 1 and 4.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities for skills development (page 107).\n4.8.2 Identification of common gases\n4.8.2.1 Test for hydrogen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for hydrogen uses a burning splint held at the open end of\na test tube of the gas. The splint relights in oxygen.\n4.8.2.3 Test for carbon dioxide\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for carbon dioxide uses an aqueous solution of calcium\nhydroxide (lime water). When carbon dioxide is shaken with or\nbubbled through limewater the limewater turns milky (cloudy).\n4.8.2.4 Test for chlorine\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for chlorine uses litmus paper. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities for skills development (page 107).\n4.8.3.6 Instrumental methods\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nElements and compounds can be detected and identified using\ninstrumental methods. Instrumental methods are accurate, sensitive\nand rapid.\nStudents should be able to state advantages of instrumental\nmethods compared with the chemical tests in this specification.\n4.8.3.7 Flame emission spectroscopy\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFlame emission spectroscopy is an example of an instrumental\nmethod used to analyse metal ions in solutions.\nThe sample is put into a flame and the light given out is passed\nthrough a spectroscope. The output is a line spectrum that can be\nanalysed to identify the metal ions in the solution and measure their\nconcentrations.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to observe\nflame spectra using a hand-\nheld spectroscope.\nStudents should be able to interpret an instrumental result given\nappropriate data in chart or tabular form, when accompanied by a\nreference set in the same form, limited to flame emission\nspectroscopy.\nWS 3.6\nMS 4a\n4.9 Chemistry of the atmosphere\nThe Earth’s atmosphere is dynamic and forever changing. The causes of these changes are\nsometimes man-made and sometimes part of many natural cycles. Scientists use very complex\nsoftware to predict weather and climate change as there are many variables that can influence this.\nThe problems caused by increased levels of air pollutants require scientists and engineers to\ndevelop solutions that help to reduce the impact of human activity.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 75"

The splint relights in oxygen.\n4.8.2.3 Test for carbon dioxide\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for carbon dioxide uses an aqueous solution of calcium\nhydroxide (lime water). When carbon dioxide is shaken with or\nbubbled through limewater the limewater turns milky (cloudy).\n4.8.2.4 Test for chlorine\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for chlorine uses litmus paper. When damp litmus paper is\nput into chlorine gas the litmus paper is bleached and turns white.\n4.8.3 Identification of ions by chemical and spectroscopic means\n(chemistry only)\n4.8.3.1 Flame tests\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFlame tests can be used to identify some metal ions (cations).\nLithium, sodium, potassium, calcium and copper compounds\nproduce distinctive colours in flame tests:\n• lithium compounds result in a crimson flame\n• sodium compounds result in a yellow flame\n• potassium compounds result in a lilac flame\n• calcium compounds result in an orange-red flame\n• copper compounds result in a green flame.\nIf a sample containing a mixture of ions is used some flame colours\ncan be masked.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to\ninvestigate flame colours.\nStudents should be able to identify species from the results of the\ntests in 4.8.3.1 to 4.8.3.5.\nFlame colours of other metal ions are not required knowledge.\nWS 2.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462.           "id": "aqa-gcse-chemistry-8462-v1.1:4-8-3",
          "code": "4.8.3",
          "title": "Identification of ions by chemical and spectroscopic means (chemistry only)",
          "kind": "section",
          "tier": [
            "Foundation",
            "Higher"
          ],
          "locator": "Official PDF pages 70-75; 4.8.3 Identification of ions by chemical and spectroscopic means (chemistry only)",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 70-75\n4.7.3.3 Amino acids (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAmino acids have two different functional groups in a molecule.\nAmino acids react by condensation polymerisation to produce\npolypeptides.\nFor example: glycine is H2NCH2COOH and polymerises to produce\nthe polypeptide\nDifferent amino acids can be combined in the same chain to\nproduce proteins.\n4.7.3.4 DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and other naturally occurring polymers\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nDNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a large molecule essential for life.\nDNA encodes genetic instructions for the development and\nfunctioning of living organisms and viruses.\nMost DNA molecules are two polymer chains, made from four\ndifferent monomers called nucleotides, in the form of a double helix.\nOther naturally occurring polymers important for life include\nproteins, starch and cellulose.\nStudents should be able to name the types of monomers from\nwhich these naturally occurring polymers are made.\n4.8 Chemical analysis\nAnalysts have developed a range of qualitative tests to detect specific chemicals. The tests are\nbased on reactions that produce a gas with distinctive properties, or a colour change or an\ninsoluble solid that appears as a precipitate.\nInstrumental methods provide fast, sensitive and accurate means of analysing chemicals, and are\nparticularly useful when the amount of chemical being analysed is small. Forensic scientists and\ndrug control scientists rely on such instrumental methods in their work.\n70 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.8.1 Purity, formulations and chromatography\n4.8.1.1 Pure substances\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn chemistry, a pure substance is a single element or compound,\nnot mixed with any other substance.\nPure elements and compounds melt and boil at specific\ntemperatures. Melting point and boiling point data can be used to\ndistinguish pure substances from mixtures.\nIn everyday language, a pure substance can mean a substance\nthat has had nothing added to it, so it is unadulterated and in its\nnatural state, eg pure milk.\nStudents should be able to use melting point and boiling point data\nto distinguish pure from impure substances.\nWS 2.2, 4.1\n4.8.1.2 Formulations\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nA formulation is a mixture that has been designed as a useful\nproduct. Many products are complex mixtures in which each\nchemical has a particular purpose. Formulations are made by\nmixing the components in carefully measured quantities to ensure\nthat the product has the required properties. Formulations include\nfuels, cleaning agents, paints, medicines, alloys, fertilisers and\nfoods.\nStudents should be able to identify formulations given appropriate\ninformation.\nStudents do not need to know the names of components in\nproprietary products.\nWS 1.4, 2.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 71\n4.8.1.3 Chromatography\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nChromatography can be used to separate mixtures and can give\ninformation to help identify substances. Chromatography involves a\nstationary phase and a mobile phase. Separation depends on the\ndistribution of substances between the phases.\nThe ratio of the distance moved by a compound (centre of spot from\norigin) to the distance moved by the solvent can be expressed as its\nRf value:\nRf = distance moved by substance\ndistance moved by solvent\nDifferent compounds have different Rf values in different solvents,\nwhich can be used to help identify the compounds. The compounds\nin a mixture may separate into different spots depending on the\nsolvent but a pure compound will produce a single spot in all\nsolvents.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how paper chromatography separates mixtures\n• suggest how chromatographic methods can be used for\ndistinguishing pure substances from impure substances\n• interpret chromatograms and determine Rf values from\nchromatograms\nWS 2.2, 3.1, 2, 3\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\n• provide answers to an appropriate number of significant\nfigures.\nMS 2a\nRequired practical 6: investigate how paper chromatography can be used to separate and tell the\ndifference between coloured substances. Students should calculate Rf values.\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 1 and 4.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities for skills development (page 107).\n4.8.2 Identification of common gases\n4.8.2.1 Test for hydrogen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for hydrogen uses a burning splint held at the open end of\na test tube of the gas. Hydrogen burns rapidly with a pop sound.\n72 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.8.2.2 Test for oxygen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for oxygen uses a glowing splint inserted into a test tube of\nthe gas. The splint relights in oxygen.\n4.8.2.3 Test for carbon dioxide\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for carbon dioxide uses an aqueous solution of calcium\nhydroxide (lime water). When carbon dioxide is shaken with or\nbubbled through limewater the limewater turns milky (cloudy).\n4.8.2.4 Test for chlorine\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for chlorine uses litmus paper. When damp litmus paper is\nput into chlorine gas the litmus paper is bleached and turns white.\n4.8.3 Identification of ions by chemical and spectroscopic means\n(chemistry only)\n4.8.3.1 Flame tests\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFlame tests can be used to identify some metal ions (cations).\nLithium, sodium, potassium, calcium and copper compounds\nproduce distinctive colours in flame tests:\n• lithium compounds result in a crimson flame\n• sodium compounds result in a yellow flame\n• potassium compounds result in a lilac flame\n• calcium compounds result in an orange-red flame\n• copper compounds result in a green flame.\nIf a sample containing a mixture of ions is used some flame colours\ncan be masked.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to\ninvestigate flame colours.\nStudents should be able to identify species from the results of the\ntests in 4.8.3.1 to 4.8.3.5.\nFlame colours of other metal ions are not required knowledge.\nWS 2.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 73\n4.8.3.2 Metal hydroxides\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSodium hydroxide solution can be used to identify some metal ions\n(cations).\nSolutions of aluminium, calcium"

GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 73\n4.8.3.2 Metal hydroxides\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSodium hydroxide solution can be used to identify some metal ions\n(cations).\nSolutions of aluminium, calcium and magnesium ions form white\nprecipitates when sodium hydroxide solution is added but only the\naluminium hydroxide precipitate dissolves in excess sodium\nhydroxide solution.\nSolutions of copper(II), iron(II) and iron(III) ions form coloured\nprecipitates when sodium hydroxide solution is added.\nCopper(II) forms a blue precipitate, iron(II) a green precipitate and\niron(III) a brown precipitate.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to make\nprecipitates of metal\nhydroxides.\nStudents should be able to write balanced equations for the\nreactions to produce the insoluble hydroxides.\nStudents are not expected to write equations for the production of\nsodium aluminate.\nWS 2.2\n4.8.3.3 Carbonates\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCarbonates react with dilute acids to form carbon dioxide gas.\nCarbon dioxide can be identified with limewater.\n4.8.3.4 Halides\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nHalide ions in solution produce precipitates with silver nitrate\nsolution in the presence of dilute nitric acid. Silver chloride is white,\nsilver bromide is cream and silver iodide is yellow.\n4.8.3.5 Sulfates\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSulfate ions in solution produce a white precipitate with barium\nchloride solution in the presence of dilute hydrochloric acid.\nRequired practical 7: use of chemical tests to identify the ions in unknown single ionic\ncompounds covering the ions from sections Flame tests (page 73) to Sulfates (page 74).\n74 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 1 and 8.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities for skills development (page 107).\n4.8.3.6 Instrumental methods\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nElements and compounds can be detected and identified using\ninstrumental methods. Instrumental methods are accurate, sensitive\nand rapid.\nStudents should be able to state advantages of instrumental\nmethods compared with the chemical tests in this specification.\n4.8.3.7 Flame emission spectroscopy\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFlame emission spectroscopy is an example of an instrumental\nmethod used to analyse metal ions in solutions.\nThe sample is put into a flame and the light given out is passed\nthrough a spectroscope. The output is a line spectrum that can be\nanalysed to identify the metal ions in the solution and measure their\nconcentrations.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to observe\nflame spectra using a hand-\nheld spectroscope.\nStudents should be able to interpret an instrumental result given\nappropriate data in chart or tabular form, when accompanied by a\nreference set in the same form, limited to flame emission\nspectroscopy.\nWS 3.6\nMS 4a\n4.9 Chemistry of the atmosphere\nThe Earth’s atmosphere is dynamic and forever changing. The causes of these changes are\nsometimes man-made and sometimes part of many natural cycles. Scientists use very complex\nsoftware to predict weather and climate change as there are many variables that can influence this.\nThe problems caused by increased levels of air pollutants require scientists and engineers to\ndevelop solutions that help to reduce the impact of human activity.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 75"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 70-75\nInstrumental methods provide fast, sensitive and accurate means of analysing chemicals, and are\nparticularly useful when the amount of chemical being analysed is small. Forensic scientists and\ndrug control scientists rely on such instrumental methods in their work.\n70 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.8.1 Purity, formulations and chromatography\n4.8.1.1 Pure substances\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nIn chemistry, a pure substance is a single element or compound,\nnot mixed with any other substance.\nPure elements and compounds melt and boil at specific\ntemperatures. Melting point and boiling point data can be used to\ndistinguish pure substances from mixtures.\nIn everyday language, a pure substance can mean a substance\nthat has had nothing added to it, so it is unadulterated and in its\nnatural state, eg pure milk.\nStudents should be able to use melting point and boiling point data\nto distinguish pure from impure substances.\nWS 2.2, 4.1\n4.8.1.2 Formulations\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nA formulation is a mixture that has been designed as a useful\nproduct. Many products are complex mixtures in which each\nchemical has a particular purpose. Formulations are made by\nmixing the components in carefully measured quantities to ensure\nthat the product has the required properties. Formulations include\nfuels, cleaning agents, paints, medicines, alloys, fertilisers and\nfoods.\nStudents should be able to identify formulations given appropriate\ninformation.\nStudents do not need to know the names of components in\nproprietary products.\nWS 1.4, 2.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 71\n4.8.1.3 Chromatography\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nChromatography can be used to separate mixtures and can give\ninformation to help identify substances. Chromatography involves a\nstationary phase and a mobile phase. Separation depends on the\ndistribution of substances between the phases.\nThe ratio of the distance moved by a compound (centre of spot from\norigin) to the distance moved by the solvent can be expressed as its\nRf value:\nRf = distance moved by substance\ndistance moved by solvent\nDifferent compounds have different Rf values in different solvents,\nwhich can be used to help identify the compounds. The compounds\nin a mixture may separate into different spots depending on the\nsolvent but a pure compound will produce a single spot in all\nsolvents.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how paper chromatography separates mixtures\n• suggest how chromatographic methods can be used for\ndistinguishing pure substances from impure substances\n• interpret chromatograms and determine Rf values from\nchromatograms\nWS 2.2, 3.1, 2, 3\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\n• provide answers to an appropriate number of significant\nfigures.\nMS 2a\nRequired practical 6: investigate how paper chromatography can be used to separate and tell the\ndifference between coloured substances. Students should calculate Rf values.\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 1 and 4.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities for skills development (page 107).\n4.8.2 Identification of common gases\n4.8.2.1 Test for hydrogen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for hydrogen uses a burning splint held at the open end of\na test tube of the gas. Hydrogen burns rapidly with a pop sound.\n72 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.8.2.2 Test for oxygen\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe test for oxygen uses a glowing splint inserted into a test tube of\nthe gas.           "locator": "Official PDF pages 75-80; 4.9 Chemistry of the atmosphere",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 75-80\nChemistry of the atmosphere\nThe Earth’s atmosphere is dynamic and forever changing. The causes of these changes are\nsometimes man-made and sometimes part of many natural cycles. Scientists use very complex\nsoftware to predict weather and climate change as there are many variables that can influence this.\nThe problems caused by increased levels of air pollutants require scientists and engineers to\ndevelop solutions that help to reduce the impact of human activity.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 75\n4.9.1 The composition and evolution of the Earth's atmosphere\n4.9.1.1 The proportions of different gases in the atmosphere\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFor 200 million years, the proportions of different gases in the\natmosphere have been much the same as they are today:\n• about four-fifths (approximately 80%) nitrogen\n• about one-fifth (approximately 20%) oxygen\n• small proportions of various other gases, including carbon\ndioxide, water vapour and noble gases.\nMS 1c\nTo use ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n4.9.1.2 The Earth's early atmosphere\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nTheories about what was in the Earth’s early atmosphere and how\nthe atmosphere was formed have changed and developed over\ntime. Evidence for the early atmosphere is limited because of the\ntime scale of 4.6 billion years.\nOne theory suggests that during the first billion years of the Earth’s\nexistence there was intense volcanic activity that released gases\nthat formed the early atmosphere and water vapour that condensed\nto form the oceans. At the start of this period the Earth’s\natmosphere may have been like the atmospheres of Mars and\nVenus today, consisting of mainly carbon dioxide with little or no\noxygen gas.\nVolcanoes also produced nitrogen which gradually built up in the\natmosphere and there may have been small proportions of methane\nand ammonia.\nWhen the oceans formed carbon dioxide dissolved in the water and\ncarbonates were precipitated producing sediments, reducing the\namount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. No knowledge of other\ntheories is required.\nStudents should be able to, given appropriate information, interpret\nevidence and evaluate different theories about the Earth’s early\natmosphere.\nWS 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 3.5, 3.6,\n4.1\n76 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.9.1.3 How oxygen increased\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlgae and plants produced the oxygen that is now in the\natmosphere by photosynthesis, which can be represented by the\nequation:\nAlgae first produced oxygen about 2.7 billion years ago and soon\nafter this oxygen appeared in the atmosphere. Over the next billion\nyears plants evolved and the percentage of oxygen gradually\nincreased to a level that enabled animals to evolve.\nWS 1.2\nAn opportunity to show that\naquatic plants produce\noxygen in daylight.\n4.9.1.4 How carbon dioxide decreased\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlgae and plants decreased the percentage of carbon dioxide in the\natmosphere by photosynthesis.\nCarbon dioxide was also decreased by the formation of\nsedimentary rocks and fossil fuels that contain carbon.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe the main changes in the atmosphere over time and\nsome of the likely causes of these changes\n• describe and explain the formation of deposits of limestone,\ncoal, crude oil and natural gas.\nWS 1.2, 4.1\n4.9.2 Carbon dioxide and methane as greenhouse gases\n4.9.2.1 Greenhouse gases\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nGreenhouse gases in the atmosphere maintain temperatures on\nEarth high enough to support life. Water vapour, carbon dioxide and\nmethane are greenhouse gases.\nStudents should be able to describe the greenhouse effect in terms\nof the interaction of short and long wavelength radiation with matter.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 77\n4.9.2.2 Human activities which contribute to an increase in greenhouse gases in the\natmosphere\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSome human activities increase the amounts of greenhouse gases\nin the atmosphere. These include:\n• carbon dioxide\n• methane.\nStudents should be able to recall two human activities that increase\nthe amounts of each of the greenhouse gases carbon dioxide and\nmethane.\nBased on peer-reviewed evidence, many scientists believe that\nhuman activities will cause the temperature of the Earth’s\natmosphere to increase at the surface and that this will result in\nglobal climate change.\nHowever, it is difficult to model such complex systems as global\nclimate change. This leads to simplified models, speculation and\nopinions presented in the media that may be based on only parts of\nthe evidence and which may be biased.\nStudents should be able to:\n• evaluate the quality of evidence in a report about global\nclimate change given appropriate information\n• describe uncertainties in the evidence base\n• recognise the importance of peer review of results and of\ncommunicating results to a wide range of audiences.\nWS 1.2, 1.3, 1.6\n4.9.2.3 Global climate change\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAn increase in average global temperature is a major cause of\nclimate change.\nThere are several potential effects of global climate change.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe briefly four potential effects of global climate change\n• discuss the scale, risk and environmental implications of\nglobal climate change.\nWS 1.5\n78 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.9.2.4 The carbon footprint and its reduction\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe carbon footprint is the total amount of carbon dioxide and other\ngreenhouse gases emitted over the full life cycle of a product,\nservice or event.\nThe carbon footprint can be reduced by reducing emissions of\ncarbon dioxide and methane.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe actions to reduce emissions of carbon dioxide and\nmethane\n• give reasons why actions may be limited.\nWS 1.3\n4.9.3 Common atmospheric pollutants and their sources\n4.9.3.1 Atmospheric pollutants from fuels\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe combustion of fuels is a major source of atmospheric\npollutants.\nMost fuels, including coal, contain carbon and/or hydrogen and may\nalso contain some sulfur.\nThe gases released into the atmosphere when a fuel is burned may\ninclude carbon dioxide, water vapour, carbon monoxide, sulfur\ndioxide and oxides of nitrogen. Solid particles and unburned\nhydrocarbons may also be released that form particulates in the\natmosphere.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe how carbon monoxide, soot (car"

It is colourless and odourless and\nso is not easily detected.\nSulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen cause respiratory problems in\nhumans and cause acid rain.\nParticulates cause global dimming and health problems for humans.\nStudents should be able to describe and explain the problems\ncaused by increased amounts of these pollutants in the air.\nWS 1.4\n4.10 Using resources\nIndustries use the Earth’s natural resources to manufacture useful products. In order to operate\nsustainably, chemists seek to minimise the use of limited resources, use of energy, waste and\nenvironmental impact in the manufacture of these products. Chemis"

Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 77\n4.9.2.2 Human activities which contribute to an increase in greenhouse gases in the\natmosphere\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nSome human activities increase the amounts of greenhouse gases\nin the atmosphere. These include:\n• carbon dioxide\n• methane.\nStudents should be able to recall two human activities that increase\nthe amounts of each of the greenhouse gases carbon dioxide and\nmethane.\nBased on peer-reviewed evidence, many scientists believe that\nhuman activities will cause the temperature of the Earth’s\natmosphere to increase at the surface and that this will result in\nglobal climate change.\nHowever, it is difficult to model such complex systems as global\nclimate change. This leads to simplified models, speculation and\nopinions presented in the media that may be based on only parts of\nthe evidence and which may be biased.\nStudents should be able to:\n• evaluate the quality of evidence in a report about global\nclimate change given appropriate information\n• describe uncertainties in the evidence base\n• recognise the importance of peer review of results and of\ncommunicating results to a wide range of audiences.\nWS 1.2, 1.3, 1.6\n4.9.2.3 Global climate change\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAn increase in average global temperature is a major cause of\nclimate change.\nThere are several potential effects of global climate change.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe briefly four potential effects of global climate change\n• discuss the scale, risk and environmental implications of\nglobal climate change.\nWS 1.5\n78 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.9.2.4 The carbon footprint and its reduction\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe carbon footprint is the total amount of carbon dioxide and other\ngreenhouse gases emitted over the full life cycle of a product,\nservice or event.\nThe carbon footprint can be reduced by reducing emissions of\ncarbon dioxide and methane.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe actions to reduce emissions of carbon dioxide and\nmethane\n• give reasons why actions may be limited.\nWS 1.3\n4.9.3 Common atmospheric pollutants and their sources\n4.9.3.1 Atmospheric pollutants from fuels\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe combustion of fuels is a major source of atmospheric\npollutants.\nMost fuels, including coal, contain carbon and/or hydrogen and may\nalso contain some sulfur.\nThe gases released into the atmosphere when a fuel is burned may\ninclude carbon dioxide, water vapour, carbon monoxide, sulfur\ndioxide and oxides of nitrogen. Solid particles and unburned\nhydrocarbons may also be released that form particulates in the\natmosphere.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe how carbon monoxide, soot (carbon particles), sulfur\ndioxide and oxides of nitrogen are produced by burning fuels\n• predict the products of combustion of a fuel given appropriate\ninformation about the composition of the fuel and the\nconditions in which it is used.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 79\n4.9.3.2 Properties and effects of atmospheric pollutants\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCarbon monoxide is a toxic gas. It is colourless and odourless and\nso is not easily detected.\nSulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen cause respiratory problems in\nhumans and cause acid rain.\nParticulates cause global dimming and health problems for humans.\nStudents should be able to describe and explain the problems\ncaused by increased amounts of these pollutants in the air.\nWS 1.4\n4.10 Using resources\nIndustries use the Earth’s natural resources to manufacture useful products. In order to operate\nsustainably, chemists seek to minimise the use of limited resources, use of energy, waste and\nenvironmental impact in the manufacture of these products. Chemists also aim to develop ways of\ndisposing of products at the end of their useful life in ways that ensure that materials and stored\nenergy are utilised. Pollution, disposal of waste products and changing land use has a significant\neffect on the environment, and environmental chemists study how human activity has affected the\nEarth’s natural cycles, and how damaging effects can be minimised.\n80 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration"

Water vapour, carbon dioxide and\nmethane are greenhouse gases.\nStudents should be able to describe the greenhouse effect in terms\nof the interaction of short and long wavelength radiation with matter.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462.           "text": "Official PDF pages 75-80\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 1 and 8.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities for skills development (page 107).\n4.8.3.6 Instrumental methods\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nElements and compounds can be detected and identified using\ninstrumental methods. Instrumental methods are accurate, sensitive\nand rapid.\nStudents should be able to state advantages of instrumental\nmethods compared with the chemical tests in this specification.\n4.8.3.7 Flame emission spectroscopy\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFlame emission spectroscopy is an example of an instrumental\nmethod used to analyse metal ions in solutions.\nThe sample is put into a flame and the light given out is passed\nthrough a spectroscope. The output is a line spectrum that can be\nanalysed to identify the metal ions in the solution and measure their\nconcentrations.\nAT 8\nAn opportunity to observe\nflame spectra using a hand-\nheld spectroscope.\nStudents should be able to interpret an instrumental result given\nappropriate data in chart or tabular form, when accompanied by a\nreference set in the same form, limited to flame emission\nspectroscopy.\nWS 3.6\nMS 4a\n4.9 Chemistry of the atmosphere\nThe Earth’s atmosphere is dynamic and forever changing. The causes of these changes are\nsometimes man-made and sometimes part of many natural cycles. Scientists use very complex\nsoftware to predict weather and climate change as there are many variables that can influence this.\nThe problems caused by increased levels of air pollutants require scientists and engineers to\ndevelop solutions that help to reduce the impact of human activity.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 75\n4.9.1 The composition and evolution of the Earth's atmosphere\n4.9.1.1 The proportions of different gases in the atmosphere\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nFor 200 million years, the proportions of different gases in the\natmosphere have been much the same as they are today:\n• about four-fifths (approximately 80%) nitrogen\n• about one-fifth (approximately 20%) oxygen\n• small proportions of various other gases, including carbon\ndioxide, water vapour and noble gases.\nMS 1c\nTo use ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n4.9.1.2 The Earth's early atmosphere\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nTheories about what was in the Earth’s early atmosphere and how\nthe atmosphere was formed have changed and developed over\ntime. Evidence for the early atmosphere is limited because of the\ntime scale of 4.6 billion years.\nOne theory suggests that during the first billion years of the Earth’s\nexistence there was intense volcanic activity that released gases\nthat formed the early atmosphere and water vapour that condensed\nto form the oceans. At the start of this period the Earth’s\natmosphere may have been like the atmospheres of Mars and\nVenus today, consisting of mainly carbon dioxide with little or no\noxygen gas.\nVolcanoes also produced nitrogen which gradually built up in the\natmosphere and there may have been small proportions of methane\nand ammonia.\nWhen the oceans formed carbon dioxide dissolved in the water and\ncarbonates were precipitated producing sediments, reducing the\namount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. No knowledge of other\ntheories is required.\nStudents should be able to, given appropriate information, interpret\nevidence and evaluate different theories about the Earth’s early\natmosphere.\nWS 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 3.5, 3.6,\n4.1\n76 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.9.1.3 How oxygen increased\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlgae and plants produced the oxygen that is now in the\natmosphere by photosynthesis, which can be represented by the\nequation:\nAlgae first produced oxygen about 2.7 billion years ago and soon\nafter this oxygen appeared in the atmosphere. Over the next billion\nyears plants evolved and the percentage of oxygen gradually\nincreased to a level that enabled animals to evolve.\nWS 1.2\nAn opportunity to show that\naquatic plants produce\noxygen in daylight.\n4.9.1.4 How carbon dioxide decreased\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nAlgae and plants decreased the percentage of carbon dioxide in the\natmosphere by photosynthesis.\nCarbon dioxide was also decreased by the formation of\nsedimentary rocks and fossil fuels that contain carbon.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe the main changes in the atmosphere over time and\nsome of the likely causes of these changes\n• describe and explain the formation of deposits of limestone,\ncoal, crude oil and natural gas.\nWS 1.2, 4.1\n4.9.2 Carbon dioxide and methane as greenhouse gases\n4.9.2.1 Greenhouse gases\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nGreenhouse gases in the atmosphere maintain temperatures on\nEarth high enough to support life. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 79\n4.9.3.2 Properties and effects of atmospheric pollutants\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCarbon monoxide is a toxic gas. It is colourless and odourless and\nso is not easily detected.\nSulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen cause respiratory problems in\nhumans and cause acid rain.\nParticulates cause global dimming and health problems for humans.\nStudents should be able to describe and explain the problems\ncaused by increased amounts of these pollutants in the air.\nWS 1.4\n4.10 Using resources\nIndustries use the Earth’s natural resources to manufacture useful products. In order to operate\nsustainably, chemists seek to minimise the use of limited resources, use of energy, waste and\nenvironmental impact in the manufacture of these products. Chemists also aim to develop ways of\ndisposing of products at the end of their useful life in ways that ensure that materials and stored\nenergy are utilised. Pollution, disposal of waste products and changing land use has a significant\neffect on the environment, and environmental chemists study how human activity has affected the\nEarth’s natural cycles, and how damaging effects can be minimised.\n80 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration"

          "title": "The carbon footprint and its reduction",
          "kind": "topic",

          "locator": "Official PDF pages 75-80; 4.9.2.4 The carbon footprint and its reduction",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 75-80\nThe carbon footprint and its reduction\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe carbon footprint is the total amount of carbon dioxide and other\ngreenhouse gases emitted over the full life cycle of a product,\nservice or event.\nThe carbon footprint can be reduced by reducing emissions of\ncarbon dioxide and methane.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe actions to reduce emissions of carbon dioxide and\nmethane\n• give reasons why actions may be limited.\nWS 1.3\n4.9.3 Common atmospheric pollutants and their sources\n4.9.3.1 Atmospheric pollutants from fuels\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe combustion of fuels is a major source of atmospheric\npollutants.\nMost fuels, including coal, contain carbon and/or hydrogen and may\nalso contain some sulfur.\nThe gases released into the atmosphere when a fuel is burned may\ninclude carbon dioxide, water vapour, carbon monoxide, sulfur\ndioxide and oxides of nitrogen. Solid particles and unburned\nhydrocarbons may also be released that form particulates in the\natmosphere.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe how carbon monoxide, soot (carbon particles), sulfur\ndioxide and oxides of nitrogen are produced by burning fuels\n• predict the products of combustion of a fuel given appropriate\ninformation about the composition of the fuel and the\nconditions in which it is used.\nWS 1.2\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards.
