          "locator": "Official PDF pages 80-89; 4.10 Using resources",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 80-89\nUsing resources\nIndustries use the Earth’s natural resources to manufacture useful products. In order to operate\nsustainably, chemists seek to minimise the use of limited resources, use of energy, waste and\nenvironmental impact in the manufacture of these products. Chemists also aim to develop ways of\ndisposing of products at the end of their useful life in ways that ensure that materials and stored\nenergy are utilised. Pollution, disposal of waste products and changing land use has a significant\neffect on the environment, and environmental chemists study how human activity has affected the\nEarth’s natural cycles, and how damaging effects can be minimised.\n80 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.1 Using the Earth's resources and obtaining potable water\n4.10.1.1 Using the Earth's resources and sustainable development\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nHumans use the Earth’s resources to provide warmth, shelter, food\nand transport.\nNatural resources, supplemented by agriculture, provide food,\ntimber, clothing and fuels.\nFinite resources from the Earth, oceans and atmosphere are\nprocessed to provide energy and materials.\nChemistry plays an important role in improving agricultural and\nindustrial processes to provide new products and in sustainable\ndevelopment, which is development that meets the needs of current\ngenerations without compromising the ability of future generations\nto meet their own needs.\nStudents should be able to:\n• state examples of natural products that are supplemented or\nreplaced by agricultural and synthetic products\n• distinguish between finite and renewable resources given\nappropriate information.\nStudents should be able to:\n• extract and interpret information about resources from charts,\ngraphs and tables\nWS 3.2\nMS 2c, 4a\n• use orders of magnitude to evaluate the significance of data. MS 2h\nTranslate information\nbetween graphical and\nnumeric form.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 81\n4.10.1.2 Potable water\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWater of appropriate quality is essential for life. For humans,\ndrinking water should have sufficiently low levels of dissolved salts\nand microbes. Water that is safe to drink is called potable water.\nPotable water is not pure water in the chemical sense because it\ncontains dissolved substances.\nThe methods used to produce potable water depend on available\nsupplies of water and local conditions.\nIn the United Kingdom (UK), rain provides water with low levels of\ndissolved substances (fresh water) that collects in the ground and in\nlakes and rivers, and most potable water is produced by:\n• choosing an appropriate source of fresh water\n• passing the water through filter beds\n• sterilising.\nSterilising agents used for potable water include chlorine, ozone or\nultraviolet light.\nIf supplies of fresh water are limited, desalination of salty water or\nsea water may be required. Desalination can be done by distillation\nor by processes that use membranes such as reverse osmosis.\nThese processes require large amounts of energy.\nStudents should be able to:\n• distinguish between potable water and pure water\n• describe the differences in treatment of ground water and\nsalty water\n• give reasons for the steps used to produce potable water.\nRequired practical 8: analysis and purification of water samples from different sources, including\npH, dissolved solids and distillation.\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 2, 3 and 4.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities and skills development (page 107).\n82 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.1.3 Waste water treatment\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nUrban lifestyles and industrial processes produce large amounts of\nwaste water that require treatment before being released into the\nenvironment. Sewage and agricultural waste water require removal\nof organic matter and harmful microbes. Industrial waste water may\nrequire removal of organic matter and harmful chemicals.\nSewage treatment includes:\n• screening and grit removal\n• sedimentation to produce sewage sludge and effluent\n• anaerobic digestion of sewage sludge\n• aerobic biological treatment of effluent.\nStudents should be able to comment on the relative ease of\nobtaining potable water from waste, ground and salt water.\n4.10.1.4 Alternative methods of extracting metals (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe Earth’s resources of metal ores are limited.\nCopper ores are becoming scarce and new ways of extracting\ncopper from low-grade ores include phytomining, and bioleaching.\nThese methods avoid traditional mining methods of digging, moving\nand disposing of large amounts of rock.\nPhytomining uses plants to absorb metal compounds. The plants\nare harvested and then burned to produce ash that contains metal\ncompounds.\nBioleaching uses bacteria to produce leachate solutions that\ncontain metal compounds.\nThe metal compounds can be processed to obtain the metal. For\nexample, copper can be obtained from solutions of copper\ncompounds by displacement using scrap iron or by electrolysis.\nStudents should be able to evaluate alternative biological methods\nof metal extraction, given appropriate information.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 83\n4.10.2 Life cycle assessment and recycling\n4.10.2.1 Life cycle assessment\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nLife cycle assessments (LCAs) are carried out to assess the\nenvironmental impact of products in each of these stages:\n• extracting and processing raw materials\n• manufacturing and packaging\n• use and operation during its lifetime\n• disposal at the end of its useful life, including transport and\ndistribution at each stage.\nUse of water, resources, energy sources and production of some\nwastes can be fairly easily quantified. Allocating numerical values to\npollutant effects is less straightforward and requires value\njudgements, so LCA is not a purely objective process.\nSelective or abbreviated LCAs can be devised to evaluate a product\nbut these can be misused to reach pre-determined conclusions, eg\nin support of claims for advertising purposes.\nStudents should be able to carry out simple comparative LCAs for\nshopping bags made from plastic and paper.\nWS 1.3, 4, 5\nLCAs should be done as a\ncomparison of the impact\non the environment of the\nstages in the life of a\nproduct, and only quantified\nwhere data is readily\navailable for energy, water,\nresources and wastes.\nInterpret LCAs of materials\nor products given\nappr"

Allocating numerical values to\npollutant effects is less straightforward and requires value\njudgements, so LCA is not a purely objective process.\nSelective or abbreviated LCAs can be devised to evaluate a product\nbut these can be misused to reach pre-determined conclusions, eg\nin support of claims for advertising purposes.\nStudents should be able to carry out simple comparative LCAs for\nshopping bags made from plastic and paper.\nWS 1.3, 4, 5\nLCAs should be done as a\ncomparison of the impact\non the environment of the\nstages in the life of a\nproduct, and only quantified\nwhere data is readily\navailable for energy, water,\nresources and wastes.\nInterpret LCAs of materials\nor products given\nappropriate information.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 4a\nTranslate information\nbetween graphical and\nnumeric form.\n84 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.2.2 Ways of reducing the use of resources\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe reduction in use, reuse and recycling of materials by end users\nreduces the use of limited resources, use of energy sources, waste\nand environmental impacts.\nMetals, glass, building materials, clay ceramics and most plastics\nare produced from limited raw materials. Much of the energy for the\nprocesses comes from limited resources. Obtai"

          "text": "Official PDF pages 80-89\npotable water\n4.10.1.1 Using the Earth's resources and sustainable development\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nHumans use the Earth’s resources to provide warmth, shelter, food\nand transport.\nNatural resources, supplemented by agriculture, provide food,\ntimber, clothing and fuels.\nFinite resources from the Earth, oceans and atmosphere are\nprocessed to provide energy and materials.\nChemistry plays an important role in improving agricultural and\nindustrial processes to provide new products and in sustainable\ndevelopment, which is development that meets the needs of current\ngenerations without compromising the ability of future generations\nto meet their own needs.\nStudents should be able to:\n• state examples of natural products that are supplemented or\nreplaced by agricultural and synthetic products\n• distinguish between finite and renewable resources given\nappropriate information.\nStudents should be able to:\n• extract and interpret information about resources from charts,\ngraphs and tables\nWS 3.2\nMS 2c, 4a\n• use orders of magnitude to evaluate the significance of data. MS 2h\nTranslate information\nbetween graphical and\nnumeric form.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 81\n4.10.1.2 Potable water\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWater of appropriate quality is essential for life. For humans,\ndrinking water should have sufficiently low levels of dissolved salts\nand microbes. Water that is safe to drink is called potable water.\nPotable water is not pure water in the chemical sense because it\ncontains dissolved substances.\nThe methods used to produce potable water depend on available\nsupplies of water and local conditions.\nIn the United Kingdom (UK), rain provides water with low levels of\ndissolved substances (fresh water) that collects in the ground and in\nlakes and rivers, and most potable water is produced by:\n• choosing an appropriate source of fresh water\n• passing the water through filter beds\n• sterilising.\nSterilising agents used for potable water include chlorine, ozone or\nultraviolet light.\nIf supplies of fresh water are limited, desalination of salty water or\nsea water may be required. Desalination can be done by distillation\nor by processes that use membranes such as reverse osmosis.\nThese processes require large amounts of energy.\nStudents should be able to:\n• distinguish between potable water and pure water\n• describe the differences in treatment of ground water and\nsalty water\n• give reasons for the steps used to produce potable water.\nRequired practical 8: analysis and purification of water samples from different sources, including\npH, dissolved solids and distillation.\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 2, 3 and 4.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities and skills development (page 107).\n82 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.1.3 Waste water treatment\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nUrban lifestyles and industrial processes produce large amounts of\nwaste water that require treatment before being released into the\nenvironment. Sewage and agricultural waste water require removal\nof organic matter and harmful microbes. Industrial waste water may\nrequire removal of organic matter and harmful chemicals.\nSewage treatment includes:\n• screening and grit removal\n• sedimentation to produce sewage sludge and effluent\n• anaerobic digestion of sewage sludge\n• aerobic biological treatment of effluent.\nStudents should be able to comment on the relative ease of\nobtaining potable water from waste, ground and salt water.\n4.10.1.4 Alternative methods of extracting metals (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe Earth’s resources of metal ores are limited.\nCopper ores are becoming scarce and new ways of extracting\ncopper from low-grade ores include phytomining, and bioleaching.\nThese methods avoid traditional mining methods of digging, moving\nand disposing of large amounts of rock.\nPhytomining uses plants to absorb metal compounds. The plants\nare harvested and then burned to produce ash that contains metal\ncompounds.\nBioleaching uses bacteria to produce leachate solutions that\ncontain metal compounds.\nThe metal compounds can be processed to obtain the metal. For\nexample, copper can be obtained from solutions of copper\ncompounds by displacement using scrap iron or by electrolysis.\nStudents should be able to evaluate alternative biological methods\nof metal extraction, given appropriate information.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 83\n4.10.2 Life cycle assessment and recycling\n4.10.2.1 Life cycle assessment\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nLife cycle assessments (LCAs) are carried out to assess the\nenvironmental impact of products in each of these stages:\n• extracting and processing raw materials\n• manufacturing and packaging\n• use and operation during its lifetime\n• disposal at the end of its useful life, including transport and\ndistribution at each stage.\nUse of water, resources, energy sources and production of some\nwastes can be fairly easily quantified. Allocating numerical values to\npollutant effects is less straightforward and requires value\njudgements, so LCA is not a purely objective process.\nSelective or abbreviated LCAs can be devised to evaluate a product\nbut these can be misused to reach pre-determined conclusions, eg\nin support of claims for advertising purposes.\nStudents should be able to carry out simple comparative LCAs for\nshopping bags made from plastic and paper.\nWS 1.3, 4, 5\nLCAs should be done as a\ncomparison of the impact\non the environment of the\nstages in the life of a\nproduct, and only quantified\nwhere data is readily\navailable for energy, water,\nresources and wastes.\nInterpret LCAs of materials\nor products given\nappropriate information.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 4a\nTranslate information\nbetween graphical and\nnumeric form.\n84 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.2.2 Ways of reducing the use of resources\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe reduction in use, reuse and recycling of materials by end users\nreduces the use of limited resources, use of energy sources, waste\nand environmental impacts.\nMetals, glass, building materials, clay ceramics and most plastics\nare produced from limited raw materials. Sewage and agricultural waste water require removal\nof organic matter and harmful microbes. Industrial waste water may\nrequire removal of organic matter and harmful chemicals.\nSewage treatment includes:\n• screening and grit removal\n• sedimentation to produce sewage sludge and effluent\n• anaerobic digestion of sewage sludge\n• aerobic biological treatment of effluent.\nStudents should be able to comment on the relative ease of\nobtaining potable water from waste, ground and salt water.\n4.10.1.4 Alternative methods of extracting metals (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe Earth’s resources of metal ores are limited.\nCopper ores are becoming scarce and new ways of extracting\ncopper from low-grade ores include phytomining, and bioleaching.\nThese methods avoid traditional mining methods of digging, moving\nand disposing of large amounts of rock.\nPhytomining uses plants to absorb metal compounds. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 83\n4.10.2 Life cycle assessment and recycling\n4.10.2.1 Life cycle assessment\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nLife cycle assessments (LCAs) are carried out to assess the\nenvironmental impact of products in each of these stages:\n• extracting and processing raw materials\n• manufacturing and packaging\n• use and operation during its lifetime\n• disposal at the end of its useful life, including transport and\ndistribution at each stage.\nUse of water, resources, energy sources and production of some\nwastes can be fairly easily quantified. Allocating numerical values to\npollutant effects is less straightforward and requires value\njudgements, so LCA is not a purely objective process.\nSelective or abbreviated LCAs can be devised to evaluate a product\nbut these can be misused to reach pre-determined conclusions, eg\nin support of claims for advertising purposes.\nStudents should be able to carry out simple comparative LCAs for\nshopping bags made from plastic and paper.\nWS 1.3, 4, 5\nLCAs should be done as a\ncomparison of the impact\non the environment of the\nstages in the life of a\nproduct, and only quantified\nwhere data is readily\navailable for energy, water,\nresources and wastes.\nInterpret LCAs of materials\nor products given\nappropriate information.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 4a\nTranslate information\nbetween graphical and\nnumeric form.\n84 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.2.2 Ways of reducing the use of resources\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe reduction in use, reuse and recycling of materials by end users\nreduces the use of limited resources, use of energy sources, waste\nand environmental impacts.\nMetals, glass, building materials, clay ceramics and most plastics\nare produced from limited raw materials. Much of the energy for the\nprocesses comes from limited resources. Obtaining raw materials\nfrom the Earth by quarrying and mining causes environmental\nimpacts.\nSome products, such as glass bottles, can be reused. Glass bottles\ncan be crushed and melted to make different glass products. Other\nproducts cannot be reused and so are recycled for a different use.\nMetals can be recycled by melting and recasting or reforming into\ndifferent products. The amount of separation required for recycling\ndepends on the material and the properties required of the final\nproduct. For example, some scrap steel can be added to iron from a\nblast furnace to reduce the amount of iron that needs to be\nextracted from iron ore.\nStudents should be able to evaluate ways of reducing the use of\nlimited resources, given appropriate information.\n4.10.3 Using materials (chemistry only)\n4.10.3.1 Corrosion and its prevention\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCorrosion is the destruction of materials by chemical reactions with\nsubstances in the environment. Rusting is an example of corrosion.\nBoth air and water are necessary for iron to rust.\nCorrosion can be prevented by applying a coating that acts as a\nbarrier, such as greasing, painting or electroplating. Aluminium has\nan oxide coating that protects the metal from further corrosion.\nSome coatings are reactive and contain a more reactive metal to\nprovide sacrificial protection, eg zinc is used to galvanise iron.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe experiments and interpret results to show that both\nair and water are necessary for rusting\n• explain sacrificial protection in terms of relative reactivity.\nWS 2.2, 7, 3.5\nInvestigate the conditions\nfor rusting.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 85\n4.10.3.2 Alloys as useful materials\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMost metals in everyday use are alloys.\nBronze is an alloy of copper and tin. Brass is an alloy of copper and\nzinc.\nGold used as jewellery is usually an alloy with silver, copper and\nzinc. The proportion of gold in the alloy is measured in carats. 24\ncarat being 100% (pure gold), and 18 carat being 75% gold.\nSteels are alloys of iron that contain specific amounts of carbon and\nother metals. High carbon steel is strong but brittle. Low carbon\nsteel is softer and more easily shaped. Steels containing chromium\nand nickel (stainless steels) are hard and resistant to corrosion.\nAluminium alloys are low density.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recall a use of each of the alloys specified\n• interpret and evaluate the composition and uses of alloys\nother than those specified given appropriate information.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n86 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.3.3 Ceramics, polymers and composites\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMost of the glass we use is soda-lime glass, made by heating a\nmixture of sand, sodium carbona"

Borosilicate\nglass, made from sand and boron trioxide, melts at higher\ntemperatures than soda-lime glass.\nClay ceramics, including pottery and bricks, are made by shaping\nwet clay and then heating in a furnace.\nThe properties of polymers depend on what monomers they are\nmade from and the conditions under which they are made. For\nexample, low density (LD) and high density (HD) poly(ethene) are\nproduced from ethene.\nThermosoftening polymers melt when they are heated.\nThermosetting polymers do not melt when they are heated.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how low density and high density poly(ethene) are\nboth produced from ethene\n• explain the difference between thermosoftening and\nthermosetting polymers in terms of their structures.\nMost composites are made of two materials, a matrix or binder\nsurrounding and binding together fibres or fragments of the other\nmaterial, which is called the reinforcement.\nStudents should be able to recall some examples of composites.\nStudents should be able to, given appropriate information:\n• compare quantitatively the physical properties of glass and\nclay ceramics, polymers, composites and metals\n• explain how the properties of materials are related to their\nuses and select appropriate materials.\nWS 1.4, 3.5, 3.8\nCompare the properties of\nthermosetting and\nthermosoftening polymers.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 87\n4.10.4 The Haber process and the use of NPK fertilisers (chemistry\nonly)\n4.10.4.1 The Haber process\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe Haber process is used to manufacture ammonia, which can be\nused to produce nitrogen-based fertilisers.\nThe raw ma"

          "id": "aqa-gcse-chemistry-8462-v1.1:4-10-2-1",
          "code": "4.10.2.1",
          "title": "Life cycle assessment",
          "kind": "topic",
          "tier": [
            "Foundation",
            "Higher"
          ],
          "locator": "Official PDF pages 80-89; 4.10.2.1 Life cycle assessment",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 80-89\nLife cycle assessment and recycling\n4.10.2.1 Life cycle assessment\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nLife cycle assessments (LCAs) are carried out to assess the\nenvironmental impact of products in each of these stages:\n• extracting and processing raw materials\n• manufacturing and packaging\n• use and operation during its lifetime\n• disposal at the end of its useful life, including transport and\ndistribution at each stage.\nUse of water, resources, energy sources and production of some\nwastes can be fairly easily quantified. Allocating numerical values to\npollutant effects is less straightforward and requires value\njudgements, so LCA is not a purely objective process.\nSelective or abbreviated LCAs can be devised to evaluate a product\nbut these can be misused to reach pre-determined conclusions, eg\nin support of claims for advertising purposes.\nStudents should be able to carry out simple comparative LCAs for\nshopping bags made from plastic and paper.\nWS 1.3, 4, 5\nLCAs should be done as a\ncomparison of the impact\non the environment of the\nstages in the life of a\nproduct, and only quantified\nwhere data is readily\navailable for energy, water,\nresources and wastes.\nInterpret LCAs of materials\nor products given\nappropriate information.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\nMS 1d\nMake estimates of the\nresults of simple\ncalculations.\nMS 2a\nUse an appropriate number\nof significant figures.\nMS 4a\nTranslate information\nbetween graphical and\nnumeric form.\n84 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.2.2 Ways of reducing the use of resources\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe reduction in use, reuse and recycling of materials by end users\nreduces the use of limited resources, use of energy sources, waste\nand environmental impacts.\nMetals, glass, building materials, clay ceramics and most plastics\nare produced from limited raw materials. Much of the energy for the\nprocesses comes from limited resources. Obtaining raw materials\nfrom the Earth by quarrying and mining causes environmental\nimpacts.\nSome products, such as glass bottles, can be reused. Glass bottles\ncan be crushed and melted to make different glass products. Other\nproducts cannot be reused and so are recycled for a different use.\nMetals can be recycled by melting and recasting or reforming into\ndifferent products. The amount of separation required for recycling\ndepends on the material and the properties required of the final\nproduct. For example, some scrap steel can be added to iron from a\nblast furnace to reduce the amount of iron that needs to be\nextracted from iron ore.\nStudents should be able to evaluate ways of reducing the use of\nlimited resources, given appropriate information.\n4.10.3 Using materials (chemistry only)\n4.10.3.1 Corrosion and its prevention\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCorrosion is the destruction of materials by chemical reactions with\nsubstances in the environment. Rusting is an example of corrosion.\nBoth air and water are necessary for iron to rust.\nCorrosion can be prevented by applying a coating that acts as a\nbarrier, such as greasing, painting or electroplating. Aluminium has\nan oxide coating that protects the metal from further corrosion.\nSome coatings are reactive and contain a more reactive metal to\nprovide sacrificial protection, eg zinc is used to galvanise iron.\nStudents should be able to:\n• describe experiments and interpret results to show that both\nair and water are necessary for rusting\n• explain sacrificial protection in terms of relative reactivity.\nWS 2.2, 7, 3.5\nInvestigate the conditions\nfor rusting.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 85\n4.10.3.2 Alloys as useful materials\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMost metals in everyday use are alloys.\nBronze is an alloy of copper and tin. Brass is an alloy of copper and\nzinc.\nGold used as jewellery is usually an alloy with silver, copper and\nzinc. The proportion of gold in the alloy is measured in carats. 24\ncarat being 100% (pure gold), and 18 carat being 75% gold.\nSteels are alloys of iron that contain specific amounts of carbon and\nother metals. High carbon steel is strong but brittle. Low carbon\nsteel is softer and more easily shaped. Steels containing chromium\nand nickel (stainless steels) are hard and resistant to corrosion.\nAluminium alloys are low density.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recall a use of each of the alloys specified\n• interpret and evaluate the composition and uses of alloys\nother than those specified given appropriate information.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n86 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.3.3 Ceramics, polymers and composites\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMost of the glass we use is soda-lime glass, made by heating a\nmixture of sand, sodium carbonate and limestone. Borosilicate\nglass, made from sand and boron trioxide, melts at higher\ntemperatures than soda-lime glass.\nClay ceramics, including pottery and bricks, are made by shaping\nwet clay and then heating in a furnace.\nThe properties of polymers depend on what monomers they are\nmade from and the conditions under which they are made. For\nexample, low density (LD) and high density (HD) poly(ethene) are\nproduced from ethene.\nThermosoftening polymers melt when they are heated.\nThermosetting polymers do not melt when they are heated.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how low density and high density poly(ethene) are\nboth produced from ethene\n• explain the difference between thermosoftening and\nthermosetting polymers in terms of their structures.\nMost composites are made of two materials, a matrix or binder\nsurrounding and binding together fibres or fragments of the other\nmaterial, which is called the reinforcement.\nStudents should be able to recall some examples of composites.\nStudents should be able to, given appropriate information:\n• compare quantitatively the physical properties of glass and\nclay ceramics, polymers, composites and metals\n• explain how the properties of materials are related to their\nuses and select appropriate materials.\nWS 1.4, 3.5, 3.8\nCompare the properties of\nthermosetting and\nthermosoftening polymers.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 87\n4.10.4 The Haber process and the use of NPK fertilisers (chemistry\nonly)\n4.10.4.1 The Haber process\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe Haber process is used to manufacture ammonia, which can be\nused to produce nitrogen-based fertilisers.\nThe raw materials for the Haber process are nitrogen and\nhydrogen.\nStudents should be able to recall a source for the nitrogen and a\nsource for the hydrogen used in the Haber process.\nThe purified gases are passed over a catalyst of iron at a high\ntemperature (about 450°C) and a high pressure (about 200\natmospheres). Some of the hydrogen and nitrogen reacts to form\nammonia. The reaction is reversible so some of the ammonia\nproduced breaks down into nitrogen and hydrogen:\nnitrogen + hydrogen ammonia\nOn cooling, the ammonia liquefies and is removed. The remaining\nhydrogen and nitrogen are recycled.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n(HT only) Students should be able to:\n• interpret graphs of reaction conditions versus rate\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n• apply the principles of dynamic equilibrium in Reversible\nreactions and dynamic equilibrium (page 59) to the Haber\nprocess\n• explain the trade-off between rate of production and position\nof equilibrium\n• explain how the commercially used conditions for the Haber\nprocess are related to the availability and cost of raw\nmaterials and energy supplies, control of equilibrium position\nand rate.\nWS 3.5, 3.8\n88 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.4.2 Production and uses of NPK fertilisers\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCompounds of nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium are used as\nfertilisers to improve agricultural productivity. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 87\n4.10.4 The Haber process and the use of NPK fertilisers (chemistry\nonly)\n4.10.4.1 The Haber process\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe Haber process is used to manufacture ammonia, which can be\nused to produce nitrogen-based fertilisers.\nThe raw materials for the Haber process are nitrogen and\nhydrogen.\nStudents should be able to recall a source for the nitrogen and a\nsource for the hydrogen used in the Haber process.\nThe purified gases are passed over a catalyst of iron at a high\ntemperature (about 450°C) and a high pressure (about 200\natmospheres). Some of the hydrogen and nitrogen reacts to form\nammonia. The reaction is reversible so some of the ammonia\nproduced breaks down into nitrogen and hydrogen:\nnitrogen + hydrogen ammonia\nOn cooling, the ammonia liquefies and is removed. The remaining\nhydrogen and nitrogen are recycled.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n(HT only) Students should be able to:\n• interpret graphs of reaction conditions versus rate\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n• apply the principles of dynamic equilibrium in Reversible\nreactions and dynamic equilibrium (page 59) to the Haber\nprocess\n• explain the trade-off between rate of production and position\nof equilibrium\n• explain how the commercially used conditions for the Haber\nprocess are related to the availability and cost of raw\nmaterials and energy supplies, control of equilibrium position\nand rate.\nWS 3.5, 3.8\n88 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.4.2 Production and uses of NPK fertilisers\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCompounds of nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium are used as\nfertilisers to improve agricultural productivity. NPK fertilisers contain\ncompounds of all three elements.\nIndustrial production of NPK fertilisers can be achieved using a\nvariety of raw materials in several integrated processes. NPK\nfertilisers are formulations of various salts containing appropriate\npercentages of the elements.\nAmmonia can be used to manufacture ammonium salts and nitric\nacid.\nPotassium chloride, potassium sulfate and phosphate rock are\nobtained by mining, but phosphate rock cannot be used directly as\na fertiliser.\nPhosphate rock is treated with nitric acid or sulfuric acid to produce\nsoluble salts that can be used as fertilisers.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recall the names of the salts produced when phosphate rock\nis treated with nitric acid, sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid\n• compare the industrial production of fertilisers with laboratory\npreparations of the same compounds, given appropriate\ninformation.\nAT 4\nPrepare an ammonium salt.\n4.11 Key ideas\nThe complex and diverse phenomena of the natural world can be described in terms of a small\nnumber of key ideas in chemistry.\nThese key ideas are of universal application, and we have embedded them throughout the subject\ncontent. They underpin many aspects of the science assessment and will therefore b"

          "id": "aqa-gcse-chemistry-8462-v1.1:4-10-3-2",
          "code": "4.10.3.2",
          "title": "Alloys as useful materials",
          "kind": "topic",
          "tier": [
            "Foundation",
            "Higher"
          ],
          "locator": "Official PDF pages 80-89; 4.10.3.2 Alloys as useful materials",
          "text": "Official PDF pages 80-89\nAlloys as useful materials\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMost metals in everyday use are alloys.\nBronze is an alloy of copper and tin. Brass is an alloy of copper and\nzinc.\nGold used as jewellery is usually an alloy with silver, copper and\nzinc. The proportion of gold in the alloy is measured in carats. 24\ncarat being 100% (pure gold), and 18 carat being 75% gold.\nSteels are alloys of iron that contain specific amounts of carbon and\nother metals. High carbon steel is strong but brittle. Low carbon\nsteel is softer and more easily shaped. Steels containing chromium\nand nickel (stainless steels) are hard and resistant to corrosion.\nAluminium alloys are low density.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recall a use of each of the alloys specified\n• interpret and evaluate the composition and uses of alloys\nother than those specified given appropriate information.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n86 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.3.3 Ceramics, polymers and composites\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nMost of the glass we use is soda-lime glass, made by heating a\nmixture of sand, sodium carbonate and limestone. Borosilicate\nglass, made from sand and boron trioxide, melts at higher\ntemperatures than soda-lime glass.\nClay ceramics, including pottery and bricks, are made by shaping\nwet clay and then heating in a furnace.\nThe properties of polymers depend on what monomers they are\nmade from and the conditions under which they are made. For\nexample, low density (LD) and high density (HD) poly(ethene) are\nproduced from ethene.\nThermosoftening polymers melt when they are heated.\nThermosetting polymers do not melt when they are heated.\nStudents should be able to:\n• explain how low density and high density poly(ethene) are\nboth produced from ethene\n• explain the difference between thermosoftening and\nthermosetting polymers in terms of their structures.\nMost composites are made of two materials, a matrix or binder\nsurrounding and binding together fibres or fragments of the other\nmaterial, which is called the reinforcement.\nStudents should be able to recall some examples of composites.\nStudents should be able to, given appropriate information:\n• compare quantitatively the physical properties of glass and\nclay ceramics, polymers, composites and metals\n• explain how the properties of materials are related to their\nuses and select appropriate materials.\nWS 1.4, 3.5, 3.8\nCompare the properties of\nthermosetting and\nthermosoftening polymers.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 87\n4.10.4 The Haber process and the use of NPK fertilisers (chemistry\nonly)\n4.10.4.1 The Haber process\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe Haber process is used to manufacture ammonia, which can be\nused to produce nitrogen-based fertilisers.\nThe raw materials for the Haber process are nitrogen and\nhydrogen.\nStudents should be able to recall a source for the nitrogen and a\nsource for the hydrogen used in the Haber process.\nThe purified gases are passed over a catalyst of iron at a high\ntemperature (about 450°C) and a high pressure (about 200\natmospheres). Some of the hydrogen and nitrogen reacts to form\nammonia. The reaction is reversible so some of the ammonia\nproduced breaks down into nitrogen and hydrogen:\nnitrogen + hydrogen ammonia\nOn cooling, the ammonia liquefies and is removed. The remaining\nhydrogen and nitrogen are recycled.\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n(HT only) Students should be able to:\n• interpret graphs of reaction conditions versus rate\nMS 1a\nRecognise and use\nexpressions in decimal\nform.\nMS 1c\nUse ratios, fractions and\npercentages.\n• apply the principles of dynamic equilibrium in Reversible\nreactions and dynamic equilibrium (page 59) to the Haber\nprocess\n• explain the trade-off between rate of production and position\nof equilibrium\n• explain how the commercially used conditions for the Haber\nprocess are related to the availability and cost of raw\nmaterials and energy supplies, control of equilibrium position\nand rate.\nWS 3.5, 3.8\n88 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.4.2 Production and uses of NPK fertilisers\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCompounds of nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium are used as\nfertilisers to improve agricultural productivity. NPK fertilisers contain\ncompounds of all three elements.\nIndustrial production of NPK fertilisers can be achieved using a\nvariety of raw materials in several integrated processes. NPK\nfertilisers are formulations of various salts containing appropriate\npercentages of the elements.\nAmmonia can be used to manufacture ammonium salts and nitric\nacid.\nPotassium chloride, potassium sulfate and phosphate rock are\nobtained by mining, but phosphate rock cannot be used directly as\na fertiliser.\nPhosphate rock is treated with nitric acid or sulfuric acid to produce\nsoluble salts that can be used as fertilisers.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recall the names of the salts produced when phosphate rock\nis treated with nitric acid, sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid\n• compare the industrial production of fertilisers with laboratory\npreparations of the same compounds, given appropriate\ninformation.\nAT 4\nPrepare an ammonium salt.\n4.11 Key ideas\nThe complex and diverse phenomena of the natural world can be described in terms of a small\nnumber of key ideas in chemistry.\nThese key ideas are of universal application, and we have embedded them throughout the subject\ncontent. They underpin many aspects of the science assessment and will therefore be assessed\nacross all papers.\nThese ideas include:\n• matter is composed of tiny particles called atoms and there are about 100 different naturally\noccurring types of atoms called elements\n• elements show periodic relationships in their chemical and physical properties\n• these periodic properties can be explained in terms of the atomic structure of the elements\n• atoms bond by either transferring electrons from one atom to another or by sharing electrons\n• the shapes of molecules (groups of atoms bonded together) and the way giant structures are\narranged is of great importance in terms of the way they behave\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 89"

NPK fertilisers contain\ncompounds of all three elements.\nIndustrial production of NPK fertilisers can be achieved using a\nvariety of raw materials in several integrated processes. NPK\nfertilisers are formulations of various salts containing appropriate\npercentages of the elements.\nAmmonia can be used to manufacture ammonium salts and nitric\nacid.\nPotassium chloride, potassium sulfate and phosphate rock are\nobtained by mining, but phosphate rock cannot be used directly as\na fertiliser.\nPhosphate rock is treated with nitric acid or sulfuric acid to produce\nsoluble salts that can be used as fertilisers.\nStudents should be able to:\n• recall the names of the salts produced when phosphate rock\nis treated with nitric acid, sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid\n• compare the industrial production of fertilisers with laboratory\npreparations of the same compounds, given appropriate\ninformation.\nAT 4\nPrepare an ammonium salt.\n4.11 Key ideas\nThe complex and diverse phenomena of the natural world can be described in terms of a small\nnumber of key ideas in chemistry.\nThese key ideas are of universal application, and we have embedded them throughout the subject\ncontent.           "text": "Official PDF pages 80-89\n4.9.3.2 Properties and effects of atmospheric pollutants\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nCarbon monoxide is a toxic gas. It is colourless and odourless and\nso is not easily detected.\nSulfur dioxide and oxides of nitrogen cause respiratory problems in\nhumans and cause acid rain.\nParticulates cause global dimming and health problems for humans.\nStudents should be able to describe and explain the problems\ncaused by increased amounts of these pollutants in the air.\nWS 1.4\n4.10 Using resources\nIndustries use the Earth’s natural resources to manufacture useful products. In order to operate\nsustainably, chemists seek to minimise the use of limited resources, use of energy, waste and\nenvironmental impact in the manufacture of these products. Chemists also aim to develop ways of\ndisposing of products at the end of their useful life in ways that ensure that materials and stored\nenergy are utilised. Pollution, disposal of waste products and changing land use has a significant\neffect on the environment, and environmental chemists study how human activity has affected the\nEarth’s natural cycles, and how damaging effects can be minimised.\n80 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.1 Using the Earth's resources and obtaining potable water\n4.10.1.1 Using the Earth's resources and sustainable development\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nHumans use the Earth’s resources to provide warmth, shelter, food\nand transport.\nNatural resources, supplemented by agriculture, provide food,\ntimber, clothing and fuels.\nFinite resources from the Earth, oceans and atmosphere are\nprocessed to provide energy and materials.\nChemistry plays an important role in improving agricultural and\nindustrial processes to provide new products and in sustainable\ndevelopment, which is development that meets the needs of current\ngenerations without compromising the ability of future generations\nto meet their own needs.\nStudents should be able to:\n• state examples of natural products that are supplemented or\nreplaced by agricultural and synthetic products\n• distinguish between finite and renewable resources given\nappropriate information.\nStudents should be able to:\n• extract and interpret information about resources from charts,\ngraphs and tables\nWS 3.2\nMS 2c, 4a\n• use orders of magnitude to evaluate the significance of data. MS 2h\nTranslate information\nbetween graphical and\nnumeric form.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 81\n4.10.1.2 Potable water\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nWater of appropriate quality is essential for life. For humans,\ndrinking water should have sufficiently low levels of dissolved salts\nand microbes. Water that is safe to drink is called potable water.\nPotable water is not pure water in the chemical sense because it\ncontains dissolved substances.\nThe methods used to produce potable water depend on available\nsupplies of water and local conditions.\nIn the United Kingdom (UK), rain provides water with low levels of\ndissolved substances (fresh water) that collects in the ground and in\nlakes and rivers, and most potable water is produced by:\n• choosing an appropriate source of fresh water\n• passing the water through filter beds\n• sterilising.\nSterilising agents used for potable water include chlorine, ozone or\nultraviolet light.\nIf supplies of fresh water are limited, desalination of salty water or\nsea water may be required. Desalination can be done by distillation\nor by processes that use membranes such as reverse osmosis.\nThese processes require large amounts of energy.\nStudents should be able to:\n• distinguish between potable water and pure water\n• describe the differences in treatment of ground water and\nsalty water\n• give reasons for the steps used to produce potable water.\nRequired practical 8: analysis and purification of water samples from different sources, including\npH, dissolved solids and distillation.\nAT skills covered by this practical activity: 2, 3 and 4.\nThis practical activity also provides opportunities to develop WS and MS. Details of all skills are\ngiven in Key opportunities and skills development (page 107).\n82 Visit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration\n4.10.1.3 Waste water treatment\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nUrban lifestyles and industrial processes produce large amounts of\nwaste water that require treatment before being released into the\nenvironment. Sewage and agricultural waste water require removal\nof organic matter and harmful microbes. Industrial waste water may\nrequire removal of organic matter and harmful chemicals.\nSewage treatment includes:\n• screening and grit removal\n• sedimentation to produce sewage sludge and effluent\n• anaerobic digestion of sewage sludge\n• aerobic biological treatment of effluent.\nStudents should be able to comment on the relative ease of\nobtaining potable water from waste, ground and salt water.\n4.10.1.4 Alternative methods of extracting metals (HT only)\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nThe Earth’s resources of metal ores are limited.\nCopper ores are becoming scarce and new ways of extracting\ncopper from low-grade ores include phytomining, and bioleaching.\nThese methods avoid traditional mining methods of digging, moving\nand disposing of large amounts of rock.\nPhytomining uses plants to absorb metal compounds. The plants\nare harvested and then burned to produce ash that contains metal\ncompounds.\nBioleaching uses bacteria to produce leachate solutions that\ncontain metal compounds.\nThe metal compounds can be processed to obtain the metal. For\nexample, copper can be obtained from solutions of copper\ncompounds by displacement using scrap iron or by electrolysis.\nStudents should be able to evaluate alternative biological methods\nof metal extraction, given appropriate information.\nAQA GCSE Chemistry 8462. GCSE exams June 2018 onwards. Version 1.1 04 October 2019\nVisit aqa.org.uk/8462 for the most up-to-date specification, resources, support and administration 83\n4.10.2 Life cycle assessment and recycling\n4.10.2.1 Life cycle assessment\nContent Key opportunities for\nskills development\nLife cycle assessments (LCAs) are carried out to assess the\nenvironmental impact of products in each of these stages:\n• extracting and processing raw materials\n• manufacturing and packaging\n• use and operation during its lifetime\n• disposal at the end of its useful life, including transport and\ndistribution at each stage.\nUse of water, resources, energy sources and production of some\nwastes can be fairly easily quantified. Allocating numerical values to\npollutant effects is less straightforward and requires value\njudgements, so LCA is not a purely objective process.\nSelective or abbreviated LCAs can be devised to evaluate"
